title: Beyond Accuracy: Introducing a Symbolic-Mechanistic Approach to Interpretable Evaluation
summary: Accuracy-based evaluation cannot reliably distinguish genuine generalization from shortcuts like memorization, leakage, or brittle heuristics, especially in small-data regimes. In this position paper, we argue for mechanism-aware evaluation that combines task-relevant symbolic rules with mechanistic interpretability, yielding algorithmic pass/fail scores that show exactly where models generalize versus exploit patterns. We demonstrate this on NL-to-SQL by training two identical architectures under different conditions: one without schema information (forcing memorization), one with schema (enabling grounding). Standard evaluation shows the memorization model achieves 94% field-name accuracy on unseen data, falsely suggesting competence. Our symbolic-mechanistic evaluation reveals this model violates core schema generalization rules, a failure invisible to accuracy metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes mechanism-aware evaluation combining symbolic rules and mechanistic interpretability to distinguish genuine generalization from shortcuts in language models, using NL-to-SQL as a case study.
AI.motivation: Standard accuracy-based evaluation fails to distinguish between genuine generalization and shortcuts like memorization, leakage, or brittle heuristics, especially with small datasets. This creates a misleading picture of model competence.
AI.method: Proposes mechanism-aware evaluation that combines task-relevant symbolic rules with mechanistic interpretability to produce algorithmic pass/fail scores. Demonstrates using NL-to-SQL by training two identical architectures: one without schema information (forcing memorization) and one with schema (enabling grounding).
AI.result: The memorization model achieved 94% field-name accuracy on unseen data, falsely suggesting competence. However, the symbolic-mechanistic evaluation revealed this model violated core schema generalization rules - a failure invisible to standard accuracy metrics.
AI.conclusion: Mechanism-aware evaluation is essential for truly assessing model generalization capabilities, as standard accuracy metrics can be misleading when models exploit shortcuts rather than developing genuine understanding.

title: Implicit Turn-Wise Policy Optimization for Proactive User-LLM Interaction
summary: Multi-turn human-AI collaboration is fundamental to deploying interactive services such as adaptive tutoring, conversational recommendation, and professional consultation. However, optimizing these interactions via reinforcement learning is hindered by the sparsity of verifiable intermediate rewards and the high stochasticity of user responses. To address these challenges, we introduce Implicit Turn-wise Policy Optimization (ITPO). ITPO leverages an implicit process reward model to derive fine-grained, turn-wise process rewards from sparse outcome signals. Unlike volatile token-level rewards, these turn-level signals exhibit superior robustness and may utilize a normalization mechanism to further enhance training stability. We evaluate ITPO across three representative multi-turn collaborative tasks: math tutoring, document writing, and medical recommendation. Empirical results demonstrate that ITPO, when combined with PPO, GRPO, or RLOO, consistently achieves improved convergence than existing baselines. Elaborate trajectory analysis confirms that ITPO infers turn-wise preferences that are semantically aligned with human judgment. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/Graph-COM/ITPO.

AI.tldr: ITPO introduces implicit turn-wise policy optimization for multi-turn human-AI collaboration by deriving fine-grained process rewards from sparse outcome signals, improving convergence in interactive tasks like tutoring, writing, and recommendation.
AI.motivation: Multi-turn human-AI collaboration is essential for interactive services (adaptive tutoring, conversational recommendation, professional consultation), but optimizing these interactions via RL is hindered by sparse verifiable intermediate rewards and high stochasticity of user responses.
AI.method: ITPO (Implicit Turn-wise Policy Optimization) leverages an implicit process reward model to derive fine-grained, turn-wise process rewards from sparse outcome signals. Uses turn-level signals that are more robust than volatile token-level rewards, with normalization mechanisms for training stability. Combines with PPO, GRPO, or RLOO algorithms.
AI.result: Evaluated on three multi-turn collaborative tasks: math tutoring, document writing, and medical recommendation. ITPO combined with PPO, GRPO, or RLOO consistently achieves improved convergence over existing baselines. Trajectory analysis confirms ITPO infers turn-wise preferences semantically aligned with human judgment.
AI.conclusion: ITPO successfully addresses the reward sparsity problem in multi-turn human-AI collaboration by providing implicit, fine-grained turn-wise rewards that improve RL training stability and effectiveness across diverse interactive tasks.

title: Upper Entropy for 2-Monotone Lower Probabilities
summary: Uncertainty quantification is a key aspect in many tasks such as model selection/regularization, or quantifying prediction uncertainties to perform active learning or OOD detection. Within credal approaches that consider modeling uncertainty as probability sets, upper entropy plays a central role as an uncertainty measure. This paper is devoted to the computational aspect of upper entropies, providing an exhaustive algorithmic and complexity analysis of the problem. In particular, we show that the problem has a strongly polynomial solution, and propose many significant improvements over past algorithms proposed for 2-monotone lower probabilities and their specific cases.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a computational analysis of upper entropies for uncertainty quantification, showing they have strongly polynomial solutions and improving past algorithms for 2-monotone lower probabilities.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty quantification is crucial for model selection/regularization, prediction uncertainty quantification, active learning, and OOD detection. Upper entropy serves as a key uncertainty measure in credal approaches that model uncertainty as probability sets.
AI.method: The paper provides an exhaustive algorithmic and complexity analysis of upper entropies. It demonstrates that the problem has a strongly polynomial solution and proposes significant improvements over previous algorithms for 2-monotone lower probabilities and their specific cases.
AI.result: The research shows that the upper entropy problem can be solved in strongly polynomial time and offers substantial algorithmic improvements compared to existing methods for handling 2-monotone lower probabilities.
AI.conclusion: The computational study advances the practical application of upper entropies in uncertainty quantification by providing efficient algorithms and establishing their computational tractability, making these methods more accessible for real-world applications in machine learning and statistical modeling.

title: Synthetic Mixed Training: Scaling Parametric Knowledge Acquisition Beyond RAG
summary: Synthetic data augmentation helps language models learn new knowledge in data-constrained domains. However, naively scaling existing synthetic data methods by training on more synthetic tokens or using stronger generators yields diminishing returns below the performance of RAG. To break the RAG ceiling, we introduce Synthetic Mixed Training, which combines synthetic QAs and synthetic documents. This leverages their complementary training signals, and enables log-linear improvements as both synthetic data volume and generator strength increase. This allows the model to outperform RAG by a 2.6\% relative gain on QuaLITY, a long-document reading comprehension benchmark. In addition, we introduce Focal Rewriting, a simple technique for synthetic document generation that explicitly conditions document generation on specific questions, improving the diversity of synthetic documents and yielding a steeper log-linear scaling curve. On QuaLITY, our final recipe trains a Llama 8B model that outperforms RAG by 4.4\% relatively. Across models and benchmarks (QuaLITY, LongHealth, FinanceBench), our training enables models to beat RAG in five of six settings, outperforms by 2.6\%, and achieves a 9.1\% gain when combined with RAG.

AI.tldr: Synthetic Mixed Training combines synthetic QAs and documents to overcome RAG limitations, achieving log-linear scaling and outperforming RAG on long-document benchmarks through improved synthetic data generation techniques.
AI.motivation: Existing synthetic data augmentation methods for language models in data-constrained domains show diminishing returns and fail to outperform Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). The RAG ceiling limits performance improvements despite scaling synthetic tokens or using stronger generators.
AI.method: Proposes Synthetic Mixed Training which combines synthetic QAs and synthetic documents to leverage complementary training signals. Introduces Focal Rewriting for synthetic document generation that explicitly conditions document generation on specific questions to improve diversity. The approach enables log-linear improvements as both synthetic data volume and generator strength increase.
AI.result: Outperforms RAG by 2.6% relative gain on QuaLITY benchmark. With Focal Rewriting, achieves 4.4% relative improvement over RAG on QuaLITY. Across models and benchmarks (QuALITY, LongHealth, FinanceBench), the method beats RAG in 5 out of 6 settings, outperforms by 2.6% on average, and achieves 9.1% gain when combined with RAG.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic Mixed Training with complementary synthetic data types can overcome the RAG performance ceiling through log-linear scaling, and strategic conditioning of synthetic document generation further enhances performance in data-constrained domains.

title: PLDR-LLMs Reason At Self-Organized Criticality
summary: We show that PLDR-LLMs pretrained at self-organized criticality exhibit reasoning at inference time. The characteristics of PLDR-LLM deductive outputs at criticality is similar to second-order phase transitions. At criticality, the correlation length diverges, and the deductive outputs attain a metastable steady state. The steady state behaviour suggests that deductive outputs learn representations equivalent to scaling functions, universality classes and renormalization groups from the training dataset, leading to generalization and reasoning capabilities in the process. We can then define an order parameter from the global statistics of the model's deductive output parameters at inference. The reasoning capabilities of a PLDR-LLM is better when its order parameter is close to zero at criticality. This observation is supported by the benchmark scores of the models trained at near-criticality and sub-criticality. Our results provide a self-contained explanation on how reasoning manifests in large language models, and the ability to reason can be quantified solely from global model parameter values of the deductive outputs at steady state, without any need for evaluation of curated benchmark datasets through inductive output for reasoning and comprehension.

AI.tldr: PLDR-LLMs trained at self-organized criticality exhibit reasoning capabilities, with deductive outputs showing properties similar to second-order phase transitions, allowing reasoning quantification via order parameters without benchmark evaluation.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to explain how reasoning emerges in large language models and provide a way to quantify reasoning capabilities without relying on curated benchmark datasets, which typically require inductive outputs for evaluation.
AI.method: Training PLDR-LLMs at self-organized criticality, analyzing deductive outputs at inference time using statistical physics concepts (phase transitions, correlation length, scaling functions), and defining an order parameter from global statistics of deductive output parameters.
AI.result: At criticality, deductive outputs reach a metastable steady state showing properties of second-order phase transitions. Reasoning capabilities are optimal when the order parameter is near zero at criticality, supported by benchmark scores comparing near-critical and sub-critical models.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning in LLMs can be explained through self-organized criticality and quantified using order parameters from deductive outputs, providing a self-contained framework that doesn't require benchmark dataset evaluation for assessing reasoning capabilities.

title: Dual-Gated Epistemic Time-Dilation: Autonomous Compute Modulation in Asynchronous MARL
comment: 14 pages, 5 figures. Code available at: https://github.com/xaiqo/edtmappo. Related materials available on Zenodo: 10.5281/zenodo.19206838
summary: While Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) algorithms achieve unprecedented successes across complex continuous domains, their standard deployment strictly adheres to a synchronous operational paradigm. Under this paradigm, agents are universally forced to execute deep neural network inferences at every micro-frame, regardless of immediate necessity. This dense throughput acts as a fundamental barrier to physical deployment on edge-devices where thermal and metabolic budgets are highly constrained. We propose Epistemic Time-Dilation MAPPO (ETD-MAPPO), augmented with a Dual-Gated Epistemic Trigger. Instead of depending on rigid frame-skipping (macro-actions), agents autonomously modulate their execution frequency by interpreting aleatoric uncertainty (via Shannon entropy of their policy) and epistemic uncertainty (via state-value divergence in a Twin-Critic architecture). To format this, we structure the environment as a Semi-Markov Decision Process (SMDP) and build the SMDP-Aligned Asynchronous Gradient Masking Critic to ensure proper credit assignment. Empirical findings demonstrate massive improvements (> 60% relative baseline acquisition leaps) over current temporal models. By assessing LBF, MPE, and the 115-dimensional state space of Google Research Football (GRF), ETD correctly prevented premature policy collapse. Remarkably, this unconstrained approach leads to emergent Temporal Role Specialization, reducing computational overhead by a statistically dominant 73.6% entirely during off-ball execution without deteriorating centralized task dominance.

AI.tldr: ETD-MAPPO is an asynchronous MARL approach that reduces computational overhead by 73.6% through autonomous execution frequency modulation based on uncertainty measurements, without sacrificing performance.
AI.motivation: Current MARL algorithms use synchronous execution requiring neural network inferences at every frame, creating barriers for deployment on edge-devices with thermal and metabolic constraints. There's a need for more efficient execution paradigms.
AI.method: Proposes Epistemic Time-Dilation MAPPO with Dual-Gated Epistemic Trigger, where agents autonomously modulate execution frequency based on aleatoric uncertainty (Shannon entropy of policy) and epistemic uncertainty (state-value divergence in Twin-Critic architecture). Environment is structured as Semi-Markov Decision Process (SMDP) with SMDP-Aligned Asynchronous Gradient Masking Critic for proper credit assignment.
AI.result: Achieves >60% relative baseline acquisition improvements over current temporal models. Prevents premature policy collapse in LBF, MPE, and Google Research Football (115-dimensional state space). Reduces computational overhead by 73.6% through emergent Temporal Role Specialization during off-ball execution while maintaining centralized task dominance.
AI.conclusion: ETD-MAPPO enables efficient MARL deployment on edge-devices by breaking the synchronous execution paradigm through autonomous frequency modulation, achieving significant computational savings without performance degradation.

title: Safe Reinforcement Learning with Preference-based Constraint Inference
summary: Safe reinforcement learning (RL) is a standard paradigm for safety-critical decision making. However, real-world safety constraints can be complex, subjective, and even hard to explicitly specify. Existing works on constraint inference rely on restrictive assumptions or extensive expert demonstrations, which is not realistic in many real-world applications. How to cheaply and reliably learn these constraints is the major challenge we focus on in this study. While inferring constraints from human preferences offers a data-efficient alternative, we identify the popular Bradley-Terry (BT) models fail to capture the asymmetric, heavy-tailed nature of safety costs, resulting in risk underestimation. It is still rare in the literature to understand the impacts of BT models on the downstream policy learning. To address the above knowledge gaps, we propose a novel approach namely Preference-based Constrained Reinforcement Learning (PbCRL). We introduce a novel dead zone mechanism into preference modeling and theoretically prove that it encourages heavy-tailed cost distributions, thereby achieving better constraint alignment. Additionally, we incorporate a Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) loss to encourage exploration by cost variances, which is found to benefit policy learning. Further, two-stage training strategy are deployed to lower online labeling burdens while adaptively enhancing constraint satisfaction. Empirical results demonstrate that PbCRL achieves superior alignment with true safety requirements and outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines in terms of safety and reward. Our work explores a promising and effective way for constraint inference in Safe RL, which has great potential in a range of safety-critical applications.

AI.tldr: PbCRL introduces a preference-based constrained RL method with dead zone mechanism and SNR loss to better infer safety constraints from human preferences, addressing limitations of traditional BT models.
AI.motivation: Real-world safety constraints are complex and hard to specify explicitly. Existing constraint inference methods rely on restrictive assumptions or extensive expert demonstrations, which aren't realistic. BT models fail to capture asymmetric, heavy-tailed safety costs, leading to risk underestimation.
AI.method: Propose PbCRL with: 1) dead zone mechanism in preference modeling to encourage heavy-tailed cost distributions, 2) SNR loss to encourage exploration via cost variances, 3) two-stage training strategy to lower online labeling burden while adaptively enhancing constraint satisfaction.
AI.result: PbCRL achieves superior alignment with true safety requirements and outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in terms of safety and reward.
AI.conclusion: PbCRL explores a promising and effective way for constraint inference in Safe RL, with great potential in safety-critical applications.

title: Environment Maps: Structured Environmental Representations for Long-Horizon Agents
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures, accepted to ICLR 2026 the 2nd Workshop on World Models
summary: Although large language models (LLMs) have advanced rapidly, robust automation of complex software workflows remains an open problem. In long-horizon settings, agents frequently suffer from cascading errors and environmental stochasticity; a single misstep in a dynamic interface can lead to task failure, resulting in hallucinations or trial-and-error. This paper introduces $\textit{Environment Maps}$: a persistent, agent-agnostic representation that mitigates these failures by consolidating heterogeneous evidence, such as screen recordings and execution traces, into a structured graph. The representation consists of four core components: (1) Contexts (abstracted locations), (2) Actions (parameterized affordances), (3) Workflows (observed trajectories), and (4) Tacit Knowledge (domain definitions and reusable procedures). We evaluate this framework on the WebArena benchmark across five domains. Agents equipped with environment maps achieve a 28.2% success rate, nearly doubling the performance of baselines limited to session-bound context (14.2%) and outperforming agents that have access to the raw trajectory data used to generate the environment maps (23.3%). By providing a structured interface between the model and the environment, Environment Maps establish a persistent foundation for long-horizon planning that is human-interpretable, editable, and incrementally refinable.

AI.tldr: Environment Maps: A persistent, structured graph representation that consolidates heterogeneous evidence (screen recordings, execution traces) to improve LLM agent performance in long-horizon software automation tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with robust automation of complex software workflows due to cascading errors and environmental stochasticity; single missteps in dynamic interfaces lead to task failure through hallucinations or trial-and-error.
AI.method: Introduce Environment Maps - a persistent, agent-agnostic representation with four core components: Contexts (abstracted locations), Actions (parameterized affordances), Workflows (observed trajectories), and Tacit Knowledge (domain definitions and reusable procedures).
AI.result: On WebArena benchmark across five domains, agents with environment maps achieve 28.2% success rate, nearly doubling session-bound context baselines (14.2%) and outperforming agents with raw trajectory data (23.3%).
AI.conclusion: Environment Maps provide a structured interface between models and environments, establishing a persistent foundation for long-horizon planning that is human-interpretable, editable, and incrementally refinable.

title: Self-Evolving Multi-Agent Framework for Efficient Decision Making in Real-Time Strategy Scenarios
comment: 17 pages, 6 figures. Submitted to SCIS (Science China Information Science)
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional potential in complex reasoning,pioneering a new paradigm for autonomous agent decision making in dynamic settings. However, in Real-Time Strategy (RTS) scenarios, LLMs suffer from a critical speed-quality trade-off. Specifically expansive state spaces and time limits render inference delays prohibitive, while stochastic planning errors undermine logical consistency. To address these challenges, we present SEMA (Self-Evolving Multi-Agent), a novel framework designed for high-performance, low-latency decision-making in RTS environments. This collaborative multi-agent framework facilitates self-evolution by adaptively calibrating model bias through in-episode assessment and cross-episode analysis. We further incorporate dynamic observation pruning based on structural entropy to model game states topologically. By distilling high dimensional data into core semantic information, this approach significantly reduces inference time. We also develop a hybrid knowledge-memory mechanism that integrates micro-trajectories, macro-experience, and hierarchical domain knowledge, thereby enhancing both strategic adaptability and decision consistency. Experiments across multiple StarCraft II maps demonstrate that SEMA achieves superior win rates while reducing average decision latency by over 50%, validating its efficiency and robustness in complex RTS scenarios.

AI.tldr: SEMA is a self-evolving multi-agent framework for Real-Time Strategy games that combines dynamic observation pruning with hybrid knowledge-memory to achieve faster decision-making while maintaining high performance.
AI.motivation: Current LLMs face a critical speed-quality trade-off in RTS environments: inference delays are prohibitive due to expansive state spaces and time limits, while stochastic planning errors undermine logical consistency.
AI.method: SEMA uses a collaborative multi-agent framework that facilitates self-evolution through adaptive model bias calibration via in-episode assessment and cross-episode analysis. It incorporates dynamic observation pruning based on structural entropy to model game states topologically, and develops a hybrid knowledge-memory mechanism integrating micro-trajectories, macro-experience, and hierarchical domain knowledge.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple StarCraft II maps show SEMA achieves superior win rates while reducing average decision latency by over 50%, demonstrating both efficiency and robustness.
AI.conclusion: SEMA successfully addresses the speed-quality trade-off in RTS environments by combining topological state modeling with hybrid knowledge integration, enabling high-performance, low-latency decision-making in complex dynamic settings.

title: AscendOptimizer: Episodic Agent for Ascend NPU Operator Optimization
summary: AscendC (Ascend C) operator optimization on Huawei Ascend neural processing units (NPUs) faces a two-fold knowledge bottleneck: unlike the CUDA ecosystem, there are few public reference implementations to learn from, and performance hinges on a coupled two-part artifact - a host-side tiling program that orchestrates data movement and a kernel program that schedules and pipelines instructions. We present AscendOptimizer, an episodic agent that bootstraps this missing expertise by turning execution into experience. On the host side, AscendOptimizer performs profiling-in-the-loop evolutionary search to discover valid and high-performing tiling and data-movement configurations directly from hardware feedback. On the kernel side, it mines transferable optimization motifs by rewinding optimized kernels - systematically de-optimizing them to synthesize instructive "bad-to-good" trajectories - and distills these motifs into a retrievable experience bank for guided rewriting. By alternating host tuning and kernel rewriting in a closed loop, AscendOptimizer steadily expands feasibility and pushes latency down. On a benchmark of 127 real AscendC operators, AscendOptimizer achieves a 1.19x geometric-mean speedup over the open-source baseline, with 49.61% of operators outperforming their references, outperforming strong agent and search baselines.

AI.tldr: AscendOptimizer is an AI agent that optimizes Huawei Ascend NPU operators through evolutionary search for host-side tiling and mining optimization motifs from de-optimized kernels, achieving significant speedups over baselines.
AI.motivation: There's a knowledge bottleneck in optimizing AscendC operators on Huawei NPUs: few public reference implementations exist, and performance depends on coupled host-side tiling programs and kernel instruction scheduling/pipelining.
AI.method: AscendOptimizer uses an episodic agent approach: 1) Profiling-in-the-loop evolutionary search for host-side tiling/data-movement configurations, 2) Mining transferable optimization motifs by systematically de-optimizing good kernels to create "bad-to-good" learning trajectories, and 3) Alternating host tuning and kernel rewriting in a closed loop.
AI.result: On 127 real AscendC operators, AscendOptimizer achieves 1.19x geometric-mean speedup over open-source baselines, with 49.61% of operators outperforming their reference implementations, beating strong agent and search baselines.
AI.conclusion: AscendOptimizer successfully addresses the AscendC optimization knowledge bottleneck by bootstrapping expertise from hardware execution feedback and distilled optimization experience, demonstrating effective automated optimization for Huawei NPU operators.

title: Evaluating a Multi-Agent Voice-Enabled Smart Speaker for Care Homes: A Safety-Focused Framework
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly being explored in health and social care to reduce administrative workload and allow staff to spend more time on patient care. This paper evaluates a voice-enabled Care Home Smart Speaker designed to support everyday activities in residential care homes, including spoken access to resident records, reminders, and scheduling tasks. A safety-focused evaluation framework is presented that examines the system end-to-end, combining Whisper-based speech recognition with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approaches (hybrid, sparse, and dense). Using supervised care-home trials and controlled testing, we evaluated 330 spoken transcripts across 11 care categories, including 184 reminder-containing interactions. These evaluations focus on (i) correct identification of residents and care categories, (ii) reminder recognition and extraction, and (iii) end-to-end scheduling correctness under uncertainty (including safe deferral/clarification). Given the safety-critical nature of care homes, particular attention is also paid to reliability in noisy environments and across diverse accents, supported by confidence scoring, clarification prompts, and human-in-the-loop oversight. In the best-performing configuration (GPT-5.2), resident ID and care category matching reached 100% (95% CI: 98.86-100), while reminder recognition reached 89.09\% (95% CI: 83.81-92.80) with zero missed reminders (100% recall) but some false positives. End-to-end scheduling via calendar integration achieved 84.65% exact reminder-count agreement (95% CI: 78.00-89.56), indicating remaining edge cases in converting informal spoken instructions into actionable events. The findings suggest that voice-enabled systems, when carefully evaluated and appropriately safeguarded, can support accurate documentation, effective task management, and trustworthy use of AI in care home settings.

AI.tldr: Voice-enabled smart speaker system for care homes using AI speech recognition and RAG achieves high accuracy in resident identification and care category matching, with promising results for reminder recognition and scheduling, though some edge cases remain.
AI.motivation: To reduce administrative workload in care homes and allow staff to spend more time on patient care by developing and evaluating a voice-enabled AI system for supporting everyday activities, documentation, and task management.
AI.method: Safety-focused evaluation framework combining Whisper-based speech recognition with three RAG approaches (hybrid, sparse, dense). Used supervised care-home trials and controlled testing on 330 spoken transcripts across 11 care categories, focusing on resident identification, reminder recognition/extraction, and end-to-end scheduling under uncertainty with safe deferral/clarification mechanisms.
AI.result: Best configuration (GPT-5.2) achieved 100% resident ID and care category matching (95% CI: 98.86-100), 89.09% reminder recognition (95% CI: 83.81-92.80) with 100% recall (no missed reminders), and 84.65% exact reminder-count agreement for end-to-end scheduling (95% CI: 78.00-89.56).
AI.conclusion: Voice-enabled systems can support accurate documentation and task management in care homes when carefully evaluated and safeguarded, though challenges remain in converting informal spoken instructions into actionable events.

title: Relaxing Constraints in Anonymous Multi Agent Path Finding for Large Agents
summary: The study addressed the problem of Anonymous Multi-Agent Path-finding (AMAPF). Unlike the classical formulation, where the assignment of agents to goals is fixed, in the anonymous MAPF setting it is irrelevant which agent reaches specific goal, provided that all goals are occupied. Most existing multi-agent pathfinding algorithms rely on a discrete representation of the environment (e.g., square grids) and do not account for the sizes of agents. This limits their applicability in real-world scenarios, such as trajectory planning for mobile robots in warehouses. Conversely, methods operating in continuous space typically impose substantial restrictions on the input data, such as constraints on the distances between initial and goal positions or between start/goal positions and obstacles. In this work, we considered one of the AMAPF algorithms designed for continuous space, where agents are modeled as disks of equal size. The algorithm requires a strict minimum separation of $4$ agent radii between any start/goal positions. Proposed a modification aimed at relaxing the constraints and reduce this limit from $4$ to $2\sqrt{3}$. We theoretically demonstrated that the proposed enhancements preserve original theoretical properties, including the guarantee that all agents will eventually achieve their goals safely and without collisions.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an enhancement to an existing Anonymous Multi-Agent Path-finding algorithm in continuous space that reduces the required minimum separation between agents' start/goal positions from 4 agent radii to 2√3 radii while maintaining safety guarantees.
AI.motivation: Classical MAPF has fixed agent-goal assignments and discrete grid representations, limiting real-world applicability. Existing continuous-space methods impose strict distance constraints between start/goal positions and obstacles. The authors aim to relax these constraints for more practical use in scenarios like warehouse robot navigation.
AI.method: The work modifies an existing AMAPF algorithm designed for continuous space where agents are modeled as equal-sized disks. The proposed enhancement reduces the required minimum separation between any start/goal positions from 4 agent radii to 2√3 agent radii.
AI.result: The authors theoretically demonstrate that the proposed modifications preserve all original theoretical properties of the algorithm, including guarantees of eventual goal achievement, safety, and collision avoidance for all agents.
AI.conclusion: The enhanced algorithm successfully relaxes the strict distance constraints of existing continuous-space AMAPF methods while maintaining the same safety guarantees, making it more applicable to real-world scenarios where agents (like mobile robots) need to navigate in continuous space with limited separation.

title: Causal Reconstruction of Sentiment Signals from Sparse News Data
summary: Sentiment signals derived from sparse news are commonly used in financial analysis and technology monitoring, yet transforming raw article-level observations into reliable temporal series remains a largely unsolved engineering problem. Rather than treating this as a classification challenge, we propose to frame it as a causal signal reconstruction problem: given probabilistic sentiment outputs from a fixed classifier, recover a stable latent sentiment series that is robust to the structural pathologies of news data such as sparsity, redundancy, and classifier uncertainty. We present a modular three-stage pipeline that (i) aggregates article-level scores onto a regular temporal grid with uncertainty-aware and redundancy-aware weights, (ii) fills coverage gaps through strictly causal projection rules, and (iii) applies causal smoothing to reduce residual noise. Because ground-truth longitudinal sentiment labels are typically unavailable, we introduce a label-free evaluation framework based on signal stability diagnostics, information preservation lag proxies, and counterfactual tests for causality compliance and redundancy robustness. As a secondary external check, we evaluate the consistency of reconstructed signals against stock-price data for a multi-firm dataset of AI-related news titles (November 2024 to February 2026). The key empirical finding is a three-week lead lag pattern between reconstructed sentiment and price that persists across all tested pipeline configurations and aggregation regimes, a structural regularity more informative than any single correlation coefficient. Overall, the results support the view that stable, deployable sentiment indicators require careful reconstruction, not only better classifiers.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a three-stage pipeline to transform raw article-level sentiment scores into reliable temporal sentiment series by treating it as a causal signal reconstruction problem rather than a classification task.
AI.motivation: Transforming raw article-level sentiment observations from sparse news data into reliable temporal series remains an unsolved engineering problem in financial analysis and technology monitoring. Current approaches struggle with data pathologies like sparsity, redundancy, and classifier uncertainty.
AI.method: A modular three-stage pipeline: (1) aggregates article-level scores onto regular temporal grid with uncertainty-aware and redundancy-aware weights, (2) fills coverage gaps through strictly causal projection rules, and (3) applies causal smoothing to reduce residual noise. Also introduces a label-free evaluation framework using signal stability diagnostics, information preservation lag proxies, and counterfactual causality tests.
AI.result: Key empirical finding: a three-week lead-lag pattern between reconstructed sentiment and stock prices that persists across all tested pipeline configurations and aggregation regimes for AI-related news titles (Nov 2024-Feb 2026). This structural regularity is more informative than any single correlation coefficient.
AI.conclusion: Stable, deployable sentiment indicators require careful reconstruction rather than just better classifiers. The proposed causal signal reconstruction approach provides robust sentiment series that can reveal meaningful financial relationships despite sparse news data and classifier uncertainties.

title: Can LLM Agents Be CFOs? A Benchmark for Resource Allocation in Dynamic Enterprise Environments
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have enabled agentic systems that can reason, plan, and act across complex tasks, but it remains unclear whether they can allocate resources effectively under uncertainty. Unlike short-horizon reactive decisions, allocation requires committing scarce resources over time while balancing competing objectives and preserving flexibility for future needs. We introduce EnterpriseArena, the first benchmark for evaluating agents on long-horizon enterprise resource allocation. It instantiates CFO-style decision-making in a 132-month enterprise simulator combining firm-level financial data, anonymized business documents, macroeconomic and industry signals, and expert-validated operating rules. The environment is partially observable and reveals the state only through budgeted organizational tools, forcing agents to trade off information acquisition against conserving scarce resources. Experiments on eleven advanced LLMs show that this setting remains highly challenging: only 16% of runs survive the full horizon, and larger models do not reliably outperform smaller ones. These results identify long-horizon resource allocation under uncertainty as a distinct capability gap for current LLM agents.

AI.tldr: LLMs struggle with long-horizon enterprise resource allocation despite advances in agentic reasoning, revealing a distinct capability gap in strategic decision-making under uncertainty.
AI.motivation: While LLMs have enabled agentic systems for reasoning, planning, and acting on complex tasks, it remains unclear whether they can effectively allocate scarce resources over long time horizons under uncertainty. Current benchmarks focus on short-horizon reactive decisions, but enterprise resource allocation requires committing resources over time while balancing competing objectives and preserving future flexibility.
AI.method: The authors introduce EnterpriseArena, the first benchmark for evaluating agents on long-horizon enterprise resource allocation. This benchmark instantiates CFO-style decision-making in a 132-month enterprise simulator that combines firm-level financial data, anonymized business documents, macroeconomic/industry signals, and expert-validated operating rules. The environment is partially observable, revealing state only through budgeted organizational tools, forcing agents to trade off information acquisition against conserving scarce resources. Experiments were conducted on eleven advanced LLMs to test their performance.
AI.result: The benchmark proved highly challenging for current LLM agents: only 16% of runs survived the full 132-month horizon, and larger models did not reliably outperform smaller ones. The results demonstrate that long-horizon resource allocation under uncertainty represents a distinct capability gap that current LLM agents cannot effectively address.
AI.conclusion: Long-horizon resource allocation under uncertainty presents a significant challenge for current LLM agents, revealing a distinct capability gap in strategic enterprise decision-making. The EnterpriseArena benchmark provides a rigorous testbed for evaluating and advancing agent capabilities in CFO-style resource allocation tasks, highlighting the need for improved approaches to handling uncertainty, partial observability, and competing objectives over extended time horizons.

title: SCoOP: Semantic Consistent Opinion Pooling for Uncertainty Quantification in Multiple Vision-Language Model Systems
comment: Accepted to ICLR 2024 Workshop on Agentic AI in the Wild: From Hallucinations to Reliable Autonomy
summary: Combining multiple Vision-Language Models (VLMs) can enhance multimodal reasoning and robustness, but aggregating heterogeneous models' outputs amplifies uncertainty and increases the risk of hallucinations. We propose SCoOP (Semantic-Consistent Opinion Pooling), a training-free uncertainty quantification (UQ) framework multi-VLM systems through uncertainty-weighted linear opinion pooling. Unlike prior UQ methods designed for single models, SCoOP explicitly measures collective, system-level uncertainty across multiple VLMs, enabling effective hallucination detection and abstention for highly uncertain samples. On ScienceQA, SCoOP achieves an AUROC of 0.866 for hallucination detection, outperforming baselines (0.732-0.757) by approximately 10-13%. For abstention, it attains an AURAC of 0.907, exceeding baselines (0.818-0.840) by 7-9%. Despite these gains, SCoOP introduces only microsecond-level aggregation overhead relative to the baselines, which is trivial compared to typical VLM inference time (on the order of seconds). These results demonstrate that SCoOP provides an efficient and principled mechanism for uncertainty-aware aggregation, advancing the reliability of multimodal AI systems.

AI.tldr: SCoOP is a training-free uncertainty quantification framework for multi-VLM systems that uses semantic-consistent opinion pooling for better hallucination detection and uncertainty-aware aggregation.
AI.motivation: Combining multiple Vision-Language Models improves multimodal reasoning but amplifies uncertainty and increases hallucination risk due to heterogeneous model outputs. Existing uncertainty quantification methods are designed for single models, not multi-VLM systems.
AI.method: SCoOP uses uncertainty-weighted linear opinion pooling to explicitly measure collective, system-level uncertainty across multiple VLMs. It's a training-free framework that enables effective hallucination detection and abstention for uncertain samples.
AI.result: On ScienceQA, SCoOP achieves AUROC of 0.866 for hallucination detection (10-13% improvement over baselines) and AURAC of 0.907 for abstention (7-9% improvement). It adds only microsecond-level overhead compared to seconds-long VLM inference.
AI.conclusion: SCoOP provides an efficient and principled mechanism for uncertainty-aware aggregation in multi-VLM systems, advancing the reliability of multimodal AI systems with minimal computational overhead.

title: StateLinFormer: Stateful Training Enhancing Long-term Memory in Navigation
summary: Effective navigation intelligence relies on long-term memory to support both immediate generalization and sustained adaptation. However, existing approaches face a dilemma: modular systems rely on explicit mapping but lack flexibility, while Transformer-based end-to-end models are constrained by fixed context windows, limiting persistent memory across extended interactions. We introduce StateLinFormer, a linear-attention navigation model trained with a stateful memory mechanism that preserves recurrent memory states across consecutive training segments instead of reinitializing them at each batch boundary. This training paradigm effectively approximates learning on infinitely long sequences, enabling the model to achieve long-horizon memory retention. Experiments across both MAZE and ProcTHOR environments demonstrate that StateLinFormer significantly outperforms its stateless linear-attention counterpart and standard Transformer baselines with fixed context windows. Notably, as interaction length increases, persistent stateful training substantially improves context-dependent adaptation, suggesting an enhancement in the model's In-Context Learning (ICL) capabilities for navigation tasks.

AI.tldr: StateLinFormer is a navigation model with persistent recurrent memory states that outperforms fixed-context Transformers by learning from effectively infinite-length sequences
AI.motivation: Existing navigation systems face a dilemma: modular mapping systems lack flexibility while Transformer-based models are constrained by fixed context windows, limiting persistent memory across extended interactions
AI.method: StateLinFormer uses linear-attention navigation with stateful memory mechanism that preserves recurrent memory states across consecutive training segments instead of reinitializing them at batch boundaries, approximating learning on infinitely long sequences
AI.result: StateLinFormer significantly outperforms stateless linear-attention counterparts and standard Transformer baselines in MAZE and ProcTHOR environments, with persistent stateful training improving context-dependent adaptation as interaction length increases
AI.conclusion: The stateful memory mechanism enhances In-Context Learning capabilities for navigation tasks by enabling long-horizon memory retention and effective learning from extended sequences

title: GTO Wizard Benchmark
summary: We introduce GTO Wizard Benchmark, a public API and standardized evaluation framework for benchmarking algorithms in Heads-Up No-Limit Texas Hold'em (HUNL). The benchmark evaluates agents against GTO Wizard AI, a state-of-the-art superhuman poker agent that approximates Nash Equilibria, and defeated Slumbot, the 2018 Annual Computer Poker Competition champion and previous strongest publicly accessible HUNL benchmark, by $19.4$ $\pm$ $4.1$ bb/100. Variance is a fundamental challenge in poker evaluation; we address this by integrating AIVAT, a provably unbiased variance reduction technique that achieves equivalent statistical significance with ten times fewer hands than naive Monte Carlo evaluation. We conduct a comprehensive benchmarking study of state-of-the-art large language models under zero-shot conditions, including GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Grok 4, and others. Initial results and analysis reveal dramatic progress in LLM reasoning over recent years, yet all models remain far below the baseline established by our benchmark. Qualitative analysis reveals clear opportunities for improvement, including representation and the ability to reason over hidden states. This benchmark provides researchers with a precise and quantifiable setting to evaluate advances in planning and reasoning in multi-agent systems with partial observability.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GTO Wizard Benchmark, a public API and standardized evaluation framework for benchmarking AI agents in Heads-Up No-Limit Texas Hold'em against a superhuman poker agent that approximates Nash Equilibria, using advanced variance reduction techniques for more efficient evaluation.
AI.motivation: There's a need for precise, standardized benchmarks to evaluate planning and reasoning in multi-agent systems with partial observability (like poker). Existing poker benchmarks are limited, and variance in evaluation makes it difficult to get statistically significant results efficiently.
AI.method:
1. Created a public API and standardized evaluation framework for HUNL
2. Used GTO Wizard AI as the benchmark opponent (superhuman agent that approximates Nash Equilibria)
3. Integrated AIVAT variance reduction technique to achieve equivalent statistical significance with 10x fewer hands
4. Conducted comprehensive benchmarking of state-of-the-art LLMs under zero-shot conditions
AI.result:
1. GTO Wizard AI defeated previous champion Slumbot by $19.4 ± $4.1 bb/100
2. All tested LLMs (including GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Grok 4) performed far below the benchmark baseline
3. Dramatic progress in LLM reasoning over recent years was observed, but significant gaps remain
4. Qualitative analysis revealed improvement opportunities in representation and reasoning over hidden states
AI.conclusion: The GTO Wizard Benchmark provides researchers with a precise, quantifiable setting to evaluate advances in planning and reasoning for multi-agent systems with partial observability. While LLMs show progress, they remain far from superhuman performance in complex games like poker, highlighting important research directions for AI reasoning capabilities.

title: Dual-Criterion Curriculum Learning: Application to Temporal Data
summary: Curriculum Learning (CL) is a meta-learning paradigm that trains a model by feeding the data instances incrementally according to a schedule, which is based on difficulty progression. Defining meaningful difficulty assessment measures is crucial and most usually the main bottleneck for effective learning, while also in many cases the employed heuristics are only application-specific. In this work, we propose the Dual-Criterion Curriculum Learning (DCCL) framework that combines two views of assessing instance-wise difficulty: a loss-based criterion is complemented by a density-based criterion learned in the data representation space. Essentially, DCCL calibrates training-based evidence (loss) under the consideration that data sparseness amplifies the learning difficulty. As a testbed, we choose the time-series forecasting task. We evaluate our framework on multivariate time-series benchmarks under standard One-Pass and Baby-Steps training schedules. Empirical results show the interest of density-based and hybrid dual-criterion curricula over loss-only baselines and standard non-CL training in this setting.

AI.tldr: Dual-Criterion Curriculum Learning (DCCL) combines loss-based and density-based difficulty assessment for more effective curriculum learning in time-series forecasting.
AI.motivation: Curriculum Learning requires meaningful difficulty assessment measures, but existing heuristics are often application-specific and defining these measures is usually the main bottleneck for effective learning.
AI.method: Proposes DCCL framework that combines two views of instance-wise difficulty assessment: loss-based criterion (training-based evidence) and density-based criterion (learned in data representation space). This calibrates loss under consideration that data sparseness amplifies learning difficulty.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on multivariate time-series benchmarks shows density-based and hybrid dual-criterion curricula outperform loss-only baselines and standard non-curriculum learning under One-Pass and Baby-Steps training schedules.
AI.conclusion: The combination of loss-based and density-based difficulty criteria provides a more effective approach to curriculum learning for time-series forecasting, demonstrating the value of considering data sparsity in difficulty assessment.

title: Grounding Vision and Language to 3D Masks for Long-Horizon Box Rearrangement
summary: We study long-horizon planning in 3D environments from under-specified natural-language goals using only visual observations, focusing on multi-step 3D box rearrangement tasks. Existing approaches typically rely on symbolic planners with brittle relational grounding of states and goals, or on direct action-sequence generation from 2D vision-language models (VLMs). Both approaches struggle with reasoning over many objects, rich 3D geometry, and implicit semantic constraints. Recent advances in 3D VLMs demonstrate strong grounding of natural-language referents to 3D segmentation masks, suggesting the potential for more general planning capabilities. We extend existing 3D grounding models and propose Reactive Action Mask Planner (RAMP-3D), which formulates long-horizon planning as sequential reactive prediction of paired 3D masks: a "which-object" mask indicating what to pick and a "which-target-region" mask specifying where to place it. The resulting system processes RGB-D observations and natural-language task specifications to reactively generate multi-step pick-and-place actions for 3D box rearrangement. We conduct experiments across 11 task variants in warehouse-style environments with 1-30 boxes and diverse natural-language constraints. RAMP-3D achieves 79.5% success rate on long-horizon rearrangement tasks and significantly outperforms 2D VLM-based baselines, establishing mask-based reactive policies as a promising alternative to symbolic pipelines for long-horizon planning.

AI.tldr: RAMP-3D is a reactive action mask planner that uses 3D vision-language models for long-horizon box rearrangement tasks, achieving 79.5% success by predicting sequential 3D mask pairs rather than using symbolic planners or 2D VLMs.
AI.motivation: Current approaches for 3D environment planning from natural language goals struggle with reasoning over many objects, complex 3D geometry, and implicit semantic constraints. Symbolic planners have brittle relational grounding, while 2D vision-language models lack 3D understanding, creating a need for 3D-aware planning methods.
AI.method: The proposed RAMP-3D formulates long-horizon planning as sequential reactive prediction of paired 3D segmentation masks: a "which-object" mask for what to pick and a "which-target-region" mask for where to place it. The system processes RGB-D observations and natural-language task specifications to generate multi-step pick-and-place actions for 3D box rearrangement.
AI.result: RAMP-3D achieves 79.5% success rate on long-horizon 3D box rearrangement tasks across 11 task variants in warehouse-style environments with 1-30 boxes and diverse natural-language constraints, significantly outperforming 2D VLM-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: Mask-based reactive policies represent a promising alternative to symbolic planning pipelines for long-horizon 3D planning tasks, successfully bridging the gap between 3D grounding capabilities and sequential decision-making without explicit symbolic reasoning.

title: PoiCGAN: A Targeted Poisoning Based on Feature-Label Joint Perturbation in Federated Learning
summary: Federated Learning (FL), as a popular distributed learning paradigm, has shown outstanding performance in improving computational efficiency and protecting data privacy, and is widely applied in industrial image classification. However, due to its distributed nature, FL is vulnerable to threats from malicious clients, with poisoning attacks being a common threat. A major limitation of existing poisoning attack methods is their difficulty in bypassing model performance tests and defense mechanisms based on model anomaly detection. This often results in the detection and removal of poisoned models, which undermines their practical utility. To ensure both the performance of industrial image classification and attacks, we propose a targeted poisoning attack, PoiCGAN, based on feature-label collaborative perturbation. Our method modifies the inputs of the discriminator and generator in the Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (CGAN) to influence the training process, generating an ideal poison generator. This generator not only produces specific poisoned samples but also automatically performs label flipping. Experiments across various datasets show that our method achieves an attack success rate 83.97% higher than baseline methods, with a less than 8.87% reduction in the main task's accuracy. Moreover, the poisoned samples and malicious models exhibit high stealthiness.

AI.tldr: PoiCGAN: A targeted poisoning attack method for Federated Learning using feature-label collaborative perturbation via modified CGAN to generate stealthy poisoned samples with automatic label flipping.
AI.motivation: Federated Learning is vulnerable to poisoning attacks, but existing methods are easily detected by model performance tests and defense mechanisms based on model anomaly detection. This reduces their practical utility, creating a need for more stealthy attacks.
AI.method: Proposed PoiCGAN method based on feature-label collaborative perturbation. It modifies the inputs of the discriminator and generator in Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (CGAN) to influence training and generate an ideal poison generator that produces specific poisoned samples with automatic label flipping.
AI.result: Achieves attack success rate 83.97% higher than baseline methods with less than 8.87% reduction in main task accuracy. Poisoned samples and malicious models exhibit high stealthiness across various datasets.
AI.conclusion: PoiCGAN provides an effective targeted poisoning attack for Federated Learning that maintains both attack effectiveness and model performance while being stealthy enough to bypass common defense mechanisms.

title: LLMs Do Not Grade Essays Like Humans
summary: Large language models have recently been proposed as tools for automated essay scoring, but their agreement with human grading remains unclear. In this work, we evaluate how LLM-generated scores compare with human grades and analyze the grading behavior of several models from the GPT and Llama families in an out-of-the-box setting, without task-specific training. Our results show that agreement between LLM and human scores remains relatively weak and varies with essay characteristics. In particular, compared to human raters, LLMs tend to assign higher scores to short or underdeveloped essays, while assigning lower scores to longer essays that contain minor grammatical or spelling errors. We also find that the scores generated by LLMs are generally consistent with the feedback they generate: essays receiving more praise tend to receive higher scores, while essays receiving more criticism tend to receive lower scores. These results suggest that LLM-generated scores and feedback follow coherent patterns but rely on signals that differ from those used by human raters, resulting in limited alignment with human grading practices. Nevertheless, our work shows that LLMs produce feedback that is consistent with their grading and that they can be reliably used in supporting essay scoring.

AI.tldr: LLMs show weak agreement with human essay scoring, tend to over-score short essays and under-score long essays with minor errors, but produce feedback consistent with their own grading patterns.
AI.motivation: Large language models are being proposed for automated essay scoring, but it's unclear how well their scores align with human grading practices, warranting evaluation of LLM-human agreement.
AI.method: Evaluated GPT and Llama family LLMs in an out-of-the-box setting without task-specific training, comparing their essay scores with human grades and analyzing grading patterns based on essay characteristics.
AI.result: LLM-human agreement is relatively weak and varies with essay characteristics: LLMs tend to assign higher scores to short/underdeveloped essays and lower scores to longer essays with minor grammatical/spelling errors. LLM-generated feedback is consistent with their scores (praise correlates with higher scores, criticism with lower scores).
AI.conclusion: LLM-generated scores follow coherent internal patterns but rely on different signals than human raters, resulting in limited alignment with human grading. However, they produce feedback consistent with their grading and can be reliably used as supporting tools for essay scoring.

title: APreQEL: Adaptive Mixed Precision Quantization For Edge LLMs
summary: Today, large language models have demonstrated their strengths in various tasks ranging from reasoning, code generation, and complex problem solving. However, this advancement comes with a high computational cost and memory requirements, making it challenging to deploy these models on edge devices to ensure real-time responses and data privacy. Quantization is one common approach to reducing memory use, but most methods apply it uniformly across all layers. This does not account for the fact that different layers may respond differently to reduced precision. Importantly, memory consumption and computational throughput are not necessarily aligned, further complicating deployment decisions. This paper proposes an adaptive mixed precision quantization mechanism that balances memory, latency, and accuracy in edge deployment under user-defined priorities. This is achieved by analyzing the layer-wise contribution and by inferring how different quantization types behave across the target hardware platform in order to assign the most suitable quantization type to each layer. This integration ensures that layer importance and the overall performance trade-offs are jointly respected in this design. Our work unlocks new configuration designs that uniform quantization cannot achieve, expanding the solution space to efficiently deploy the LLMs on resource-constrained devices.

AI.tldr: Adaptive mixed precision quantization for LLMs that customizes quantization per layer based on hardware performance and layer importance, enabling efficient edge deployment with balanced memory, latency, and accuracy trade-offs.
AI.motivation: LLMs have high computational costs and memory requirements, making edge deployment challenging for real-time responses and data privacy. Uniform quantization methods fail to account for varying layer sensitivity to precision reduction, and memory vs. computational throughput aren't aligned.
AI.method: Proposes adaptive mixed precision quantization that analyzes layer-wise contribution and infers how different quantization types perform on target hardware. Assigns most suitable quantization type to each layer, jointly respecting layer importance and overall performance trade-offs.
AI.result: The approach unlocks new configuration designs that uniform quantization cannot achieve, expanding the solution space for deploying LLMs on resource-constrained devices.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive mixed precision quantization mechanism enables efficient edge deployment of LLMs by intelligently balancing memory, latency, and accuracy according to user-defined priorities through layer-specific quantization assignments.

title: Efficient Benchmarking of AI Agents
summary: Evaluating AI agents on comprehensive benchmarks is expensive because each evaluation requires interactive rollouts with tool use and multi-step reasoning. We study whether small task subsets can preserve agent rankings at substantially lower cost. Unlike static language model benchmarks, agent evaluation is subject to scaffold-driven distribution shift, since performance depends on the framework wrapping the underlying model. Across eight benchmarks, 33 agent scaffolds, and 70+ model configurations, we find that absolute score prediction degrades under this shift, while rank-order prediction remains stable. Exploiting this asymmetry, we propose a simple optimization-free protocol: evaluate new agents only on tasks with intermediate historical pass rates (30-70%). This mid-range difficulty filter, motivated by Item Response Theory, reduces the number of evaluation tasks by 44-70% while maintaining high rank fidelity under scaffold and temporal shifts. It provides more reliable rankings than random sampling, which exhibits high variance across seeds, and outperforms greedy task selection under distribution shift. These results suggest that reliable leaderboard ranking does not require full-benchmark evaluation.

AI.tldr: Agent evaluation is expensive; using mid-difficulty task subsets preserves rankings at 44-70% cost reduction.
AI.motivation: Evaluating AI agents on comprehensive benchmarks is expensive due to interactive rollouts with tool use and multi-step reasoning. The paper aims to determine if small task subsets can preserve agent rankings at substantially lower cost.
AI.method: The study examines agent evaluation across eight benchmarks, 33 agent scaffolds, and 70+ model configurations. It finds that while absolute score prediction degrades under scaffold-driven distribution shift, rank-order prediction remains stable. A simple optimization-free protocol is proposed: evaluate new agents only on tasks with intermediate historical pass rates (30-70%), motivated by Item Response Theory.
AI.result: The mid-range difficulty filter reduces evaluation tasks by 44-70% while maintaining high rank fidelity under scaffold and temporal shifts. It provides more reliable rankings than random sampling (which has high variance) and outperforms greedy task selection under distribution shift.
AI.conclusion: Reliable leaderboard ranking of AI agents does not require full-benchmark evaluation. Using strategically selected subsets of tasks with intermediate difficulty preserves ranking order while significantly reducing evaluation costs.

title: The Geometric Price of Discrete Logic: Context-driven Manifold Dynamics of Number Representations
summary: Large language models (LLMs) generalize smoothly across continuous semantic spaces, yet strict logical reasoning demands the formation of discrete decision boundaries. Prevailing theories relying on linear isometric projections fail to resolve this fundamental tension. In this work, we argue that task context operates as a non-isometric dynamical operator that enforces a necessary "topological distortion." By applying Gram-Schmidt decomposition to residual-stream activations , we reveal a dual-modulation mechanism driving this process: a class-agnostic topological preservation that anchors global structure to prevent semantic collapse, and a specific algebraic divergence that directionally tears apart cross-class concepts to forge logical boundaries. We validate this geometric evolution across a gradient of tasks, from simple mapping to complex primality testing. Crucially, targeted specific vector ablation establishes a strict causal binding between this topology and model function: algebraically erasing the divergence component collapses parity classification accuracy from 100% to chance levels (38.57%). Furthermore, we uncover a three-phase layer-wise geometric dynamic and demonstrate that under social pressure prompts, models fail to generate sufficient divergence. This results in a "manifold entanglement" that geometrically explains sycophancy and hallucination. Ultimately, our findings revise the linear-isometric presumption, demonstrating that the emergence of discrete logic in LLMs is purchased at an irreducible cost of topological deformation.

AI.tldr: LLMs achieve logical reasoning through topological distortion rather than linear projection, with a dual-modulation mechanism that both preserves semantic structure and creates discrete decision boundaries via algebraic divergence.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the fundamental tension between LLMs' continuous semantic generalization and the discrete decision boundaries required for logical reasoning. Prevailing linear isometric projection theories fail to explain how LLMs accomplish this, motivating an investigation into alternative geometric mechanisms.
AI.method: Used Gram-Schmidt decomposition on residual-stream activations to analyze geometric transformations. Validated across gradient of tasks from simple mapping to complex primality testing. Employed targeted vector ablation experiments to establish causal relationships and examined layer-wise geometric dynamics under various prompting conditions.
AI.result: Revealed dual-modulation mechanism: class-agnostic topological preservation prevents semantic collapse, while specific algebraic divergence tears apart cross-class concepts to create logical boundaries. Ablating divergence component collapsed parity classification from 100% to chance (38.57%). Discovered three-phase layer-wise geometric dynamics and showed insufficient divergence under social pressure leads to "manifold entanglement" explaining sycophancy and hallucinations.
AI.conclusion: Discrete logical reasoning in LLMs emerges through irreducible topological deformation rather than linear-isometric projection. The cost of logical capability is geometric distortion, with insufficient distortion explaining failure modes like sycophancy and hallucinations.

title: Learning-guided Prioritized Planning for Lifelong Multi-Agent Path Finding in Warehouse Automation
summary: Lifelong Multi-Agent Path Finding (MAPF) is critical for modern warehouse automation, which requires multiple robots to continuously navigate conflict-free paths to optimize the overall system throughput. However, the complexity of warehouse environments and the long-term dynamics of lifelong MAPF often demand costly adaptations to classical search-based solvers. While machine learning methods have been explored, their superiority over search-based methods remains inconclusive. In this paper, we introduce Reinforcement Learning (RL) guided Rolling Horizon Prioritized Planning (RL-RH-PP), the first framework integrating RL with search-based planning for lifelong MAPF. Specifically, we leverage classical Prioritized Planning (PP) as a backbone for its simplicity and flexibility in integrating with a learning-based priority assignment policy. By formulating dynamic priority assignment as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP), RL-RH-PP exploits the sequential decision-making nature of lifelong planning while delegating complex spatial-temporal interactions among agents to reinforcement learning. An attention-based neural network autoregressively decodes priority orders on-the-fly, enabling efficient sequential single-agent planning by the PP planner. Evaluations in realistic warehouse simulations show that RL-RH-PP achieves the highest total throughput among baselines and generalizes effectively across agent densities, planning horizons, and warehouse layouts. Our interpretive analyses reveal that RL-RH-PP proactively prioritizes congested agents and strategically redirects agents from congestion, easing traffic flow and boosting throughput. These findings highlight the potential of learning-guided approaches to augment traditional heuristics in modern warehouse automation.

AI.tldr: RL-guided prioritized planning framework for lifelong multi-agent path finding in warehouse automation, achieving superior throughput through learned priority assignment.
AI.motivation: Lifelong MAPF is crucial for warehouse automation but search-based solvers require costly adaptations. While ML methods have been explored, their superiority over search-based methods remains inconclusive.
AI.method: Introduces RL-RH-PP: Reinforcement Learning guided Rolling Horizon Prioritized Planning. Uses classical Prioritized Planning as backbone with RL-based dynamic priority assignment formulated as POMDP. An attention-based neural network autoregressively decodes priority orders on-the-fly.
AI.result: Achieves highest total throughput among baselines. Generalizes effectively across agent densities, planning horizons, and warehouse layouts. Proactively prioritizes congested agents and strategically redirects agents from congestion, easing traffic flow.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates potential of learning-guided approaches to augment traditional heuristics in warehouse automation. RL integration with search-based planning effectively addresses lifelong MAPF challenges.

title: Residual Attention Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Robust Multiphysics Simulation of Steady-State Electrothermal Energy Systems
summary: Efficient thermal management and precise field prediction are critical for the design of advanced energy systems, including electrohydrodynamic transport, microfluidic energy harvesters, and electrically driven thermal regulators. However, the steady-state simulation of these electrothermal coupled multiphysics systems remains challenging for physics-informed neural computation due to strong nonlinear field coupling, temperature-dependent coefficient variability, and complex interface dynamics. This study proposes a Residual Attention Physics-Informed Neural Network (RA-PINN) framework for the unified solution of coupled velocity, pressure, electric-potential, and temperature fields. By integrating a unified five-field operator formulation with residual-connected feature propagation and attention-guided channel modulation, the proposed architecture effectively captures localized coupling structures and steep gradients. We evaluate RA-PINN across four representative energy-relevant benchmarks: constant-coefficient coupling, indirect pressure-gauge constraints, temperature-dependent transport, and oblique-interface consistency. Comparative analysis against Pure-MLP, LSTM-PINN, and pLSTM-PINN demonstrates that RA-PINN achieves superior accuracy, yielding the lowest MSE, RMSE, and relative $L_2$ errors across all scenarios. Notably, RA-PINN maintains high structural fidelity in interface-dominated and variable-coefficient settings where conventional PINN backbones often fail. These results establish RA-PINN as a robust and accurate computational framework for the high-fidelity modeling and optimization of complex electrothermal multiphysics in sustainable energy applications.

AI.tldr: RA-PINN: A Residual Attention Physics-Informed Neural Network framework for accurately solving coupled electrothermal multiphysics problems in energy systems.
AI.motivation: Efficient thermal management and precise field prediction are crucial for advanced energy systems, but traditional physics-informed neural computation struggles with steady-state simulation of electrothermal coupled systems due to strong nonlinear field coupling, temperature-dependent coefficients, and complex interface dynamics.
AI.method: Proposes a Residual Attention Physics-Informed Neural Network (RA-PINN) framework that integrates unified five-field operator formulation with residual-connected feature propagation and attention-guided channel modulation to capture localized coupling structures and steep gradients.
AI.result: RA-PINN outperforms Pure-MLP, LSTM-PINN, and pLSTM-PINN across four benchmarks, achieving lowest MSE, RMSE, and relative L2 errors. It maintains high structural fidelity in interface-dominated and variable-coefficient settings where conventional PINN backbones fail.
AI.conclusion: RA-PINN establishes as a robust and accurate computational framework for high-fidelity modeling and optimization of complex electrothermal multiphysics in sustainable energy applications.

title: VehicleMemBench: An Executable Benchmark for Multi-User Long-Term Memory in In-Vehicle Agents
summary: With the growing demand for intelligent in-vehicle experiences, vehicle-based agents are evolving from simple assistants to long-term companions. This evolution requires agents to continuously model multi-user preferences and make reliable decisions in the face of inter-user preference conflicts and changing habits over time. However, existing benchmarks are largely limited to single-user, static question-answer settings, failing to capture the temporal evolution of preferences and the multi-user, tool-interactive nature of real vehicle environments. To address this gap, we introduce VehicleMemBench, a multi-user long-context memory benchmark built on an executable in-vehicle simulation environment. The benchmark evaluates tool use and memory by comparing the post-action environment state with a predefined target state, enabling objective and reproducible evaluation without LLM-based or human scoring. VehicleMemBench includes 23 tool modules, and each sample contains over 80 historical memory events. Experiments show that powerful models perform well on direct instruction tasks but struggle in scenarios involving memory evolution, particularly when user preferences change dynamically. Even advanced memory systems struggle to handle domain-specific memory requirements in this environment. These findings highlight the need for more robust and specialized memory management mechanisms to support long-term adaptive decision-making in real-world in-vehicle systems. To facilitate future research, we release the data and code.

AI.tldr: VehicleMemBench: A new benchmark for evaluating multi-user, long-term vehicle agent memory systems in executable simulation environments, testing tool use and dynamic preference management.
AI.motivation: Vehicle agents are evolving from simple assistants to long-term companions that need to continuously model multi-user preferences and handle preference conflicts and changing habits over time. Existing benchmarks are inadequate because they focus on single-user, static question-answer settings and don't capture the temporal evolution of preferences or the multi-user, tool-interactive nature of real vehicle environments.
AI.method: Created VehicleMemBench, a multi-user long-context memory benchmark built on an executable in-vehicle simulation environment. The benchmark evaluates tool use and memory by comparing post-action environment states with predefined target states, enabling objective and reproducible evaluation without LLM-based or human scoring. Includes 23 tool modules, with each sample containing over 80 historical memory events.
AI.result: Experiments show powerful models perform well on direct instruction tasks but struggle in scenarios involving memory evolution, particularly when user preferences change dynamically. Even advanced memory systems struggle to handle domain-specific memory requirements in vehicle environments.
AI.conclusion: There is a need for more robust and specialized memory management mechanisms to support long-term adaptive decision-making in real-world in-vehicle systems. The authors release the data and code to facilitate future research in this area.

title: MetaKube: An Experience-Aware LLM Framework for Kubernetes Failure Diagnosis
summary: Existing LLM-based Kubernetes diagnostic systems cannot learn from operational experience, operating on static knowledge bases without improving from past resolutions. We present MetaKube, an experience-aware LLM framework through three synergistic innovations: (1) an Episodic Pattern Memory Network (EPMN) that abstracts diagnostic patterns from historical resolutions and provides confidence-calibrated retrieval for both rapid pattern matching and guided causal exploration, (2) a meta-cognitive controller that dynamically routes between intuitive and analytical pathways based on problem familiarity, optimizing the trade-off between speed and depth, and (3) KubeLLM, a locally-deployable 8B model enhanced through domain-specific post-training on our 7,000-sample Kubernetes Fault Resolution Dataset. Evaluation on 1,873 real-world scenarios demonstrates MetaKube transforms Qwen3-8B from 50.9 to 90.5 points, approaching GPT-4.1 performance while ensuring complete data privacy. EPMN contributes 15.3% improvement through experiential learning, with continuous learning experiments showing progressive gains as the system accumulates operational knowledge. The source code and related resources are available at https://github.com/MetaKube-LLM-for-Kubernetes-Diagnosis/MetaKube.
