AI.tldr: A novel conflict-gated gradient scaling (CGGS) method is proposed to address gradient conflicts in physics-informed neural networks for epidemiological modeling, enabling stable training by dynamically modulating penalty weights based on gradient directional alignment.
AI.motivation: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) are increasingly used in mathematical epidemiology to combine noisy clinical data with compartmental models like SEIR, but training is unstable due to competing optimization objectives. Gradient vectors from data loss and physical residuals often point in conflicting directions ("gradient pathology"), causing slow convergence or optimization deadlock. Existing methods try to balance gradient magnitudes or project conflicting vectors, but these approaches have limitations.
AI.method: Conflict-gated gradient scaling (CGGS) uses cosine similarity between data and physics gradients to dynamically modulate penalty weights. Unlike standard annealing schemes that only normalize scales, CGGS acts as a geometric gate: it suppresses physical constraints when directional conflict is high (allowing prioritization of data fidelity) and restores constraints when gradients align. This provides a computationally efficient alternative to existing approaches.
AI.result: The method preserves the standard O(1/T) convergence rate for smooth non-convex objectives, which fails under fixed-weight or magnitude-balanced training when gradients conflict. CGGS autonomously induces a curriculum learning effect, improving parameter estimation in stiff epidemiological systems compared to magnitude-based baselines. Empirical results show improved peak recovery and convergence over magnitude-based methods.
AI.conclusion: CGGS provides a novel solution to gradient pathology in PINNs for epidemiological modeling, ensuring stable and efficient training through geometric gating based on gradient directional alignment. The method represents a significant improvement over existing approaches by addressing both scale and direction conflicts while maintaining theoretical convergence guarantees.

title: The Stochastic Gap: A Markovian Framework for Pre-Deployment Reliability and Oversight-Cost Auditing in Agentic Artificial Intelligence
comment: 22 pages, 5 figures, submitted to Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence
summary:
Agentic artificial intelligence (AI) in organizations is a sequential decision problem constrained by reliability and oversight cost. When deterministic workflows are replaced by stochastic policies over actions and tool calls, the key question is not whether a next step appears plausible, but whether the resulting trajectory remains statistically supported, locally unambiguous, and economically governable. We develop a measure-theoretic Markov framework for this setting. The core quantities are state blind-spot mass B_n(tau), state-action blind mass B^SA_{pi,n}(tau), an entropy-based human-in-the-loop escalation gate, and an expected oversight-cost identity over the workflow visitation measure.
  We instantiate the framework on the Business Process Intelligence Challenge 2019 purchase-to-pay log (251,734 cases, 1,595,923 events, 42 distinct workflow actions) and construct a log-driven simulated agent from a chronological 80/20 split of the same process. The main empirical finding is that a large workflow can appear well supported at the state level while retaining substantial blind mass over next-step decisions: refining the operational state to include case context, economic magnitude, and actor class expands the state space from 42 to 668 and raises state-action blind mass from 0.0165 at tau=50 to 0.1253 at tau=1000. On the held-out split, m(s) = max_a pi-hat(a|s) tracks realized autonomous step accuracy within 3.4 percentage points on average.
  The same quantities that delimit statistically credible autonomy also determine expected oversight burden. The framework is demonstrated on a large-scale enterprise procurement workflow and is designed for direct application to engineering processes for which operational event logs are available.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a measure-theoretic Markov framework to assess AI agent reliability in organizational workflows, quantifying statistical support, decision ambiguity, and oversight costs using state blind-spot metrics.
AI.motivation: Agentic AI in organizations faces reliability and oversight cost constraints when replacing deterministic workflows with stochastic policies; there's a need to determine whether AI trajectories remain statistically supported, locally unambiguous, and economically governable.
AI.method: Developed a measure-theoretic Markov framework with core metrics: state blind-spot mass B_n(tau), state-action blind mass B^SA_{pi,n}(tau), entropy-based human-in-the-loop escalation gate, and expected oversight-cost identity over workflow visitation measure. Applied to Business Process Intelligence Challenge 2019 purchase-to-pay log (251,734 cases) with log-driven simulated agents from 80/20 chronological split.
AI.result: Large workflows can appear well-supported at state level while retaining substantial blind mass over next-step decisions. Refining operational state with case context, economic magnitude, and actor class expanded state space from 42 to 668 and increased state-action blind mass from 0.0165 (tau=50) to 0.1253 (tau=1000). On held-out data, m(s) = max_a pi-hat(a|s) tracked realized autonomous step accuracy within 3.4 percentage points average.
AI.conclusion: The framework quantifies both statistical autonomy credibility and expected oversight burden, demonstrating applicability to large-scale enterprise workflows and engineering processes with operational event logs, providing a systematic approach to govern AI agent deployment in organizations.

title: Deep Neural Regression Collapse
comment: Accepted to CPAL 2026; Code will be available at https://github.com/altayunal/neural-collapse-regression
summary: Neural Collapse is a phenomenon that helps identify sparse and low rank structures in deep classifiers. Recent work has extended the definition of neural collapse to regression problems, albeit only measuring the phenomenon at the last layer. In this paper, we establish that Neural Regression Collapse (NRC) also occurs below the last layer across different types of models. We show that in the collapsed layers of neural regression models, features lie in a subspace that corresponds to the target dimension, the feature covariance aligns with the target covariance, the input subspace of the layer weights aligns with the feature subspace, and the linear prediction error of the features is close to the overall prediction error of the model. In addition to establishing Deep NRC, we also show that models that exhibit Deep NRC learn the intrinsic dimension of low rank targets and explore the necessity of weight decay in inducing Deep NRC. This paper provides a more complete picture of the simple structure learned by deep networks in the context of regression.

AI.tldr: Neural Regression Collapse (NRC) occurs across multiple layers in deep regression networks, revealing structured feature alignment with targets and intrinsic dimension learning.
AI.motivation: While Neural Collapse has been studied for classification problems, its extension to regression has been limited to the last layer. The paper aims to establish that this phenomenon occurs throughout the network (Deep NRC) and understand the structural implications for regression models.
AI.method: The authors establish Deep NRC by analyzing feature representations across different layers of neural regression models. They examine several collapse characteristics: feature subspace alignment with target dimension, covariance structure alignment between features and targets, weight-feature subspace alignment, and linear prediction error analysis.
AI.result: Deep NRC occurs across multiple layers in regression models. In collapsed layers, features lie in a subspace matching target dimension, feature covariance aligns with target covariance, layer weights align with feature subspace, and linear feature prediction error approximates overall model error. Models exhibiting Deep NRC learn intrinsic dimensions of low-rank targets.
AI.conclusion: Deep NRC reveals that regression networks learn simple, structured representations throughout their architecture, not just at the final layer. This provides a more complete understanding of the simple structures learned by deep networks in regression contexts and highlights the role of regularization like weight decay in inducing these collapse phenomena.

title: Inspection and Control of Self-Generated-Text Recognition Ability in Llama3-8b-Instruct
comment: 10 pages, 13 figs, 2 tables, accepted as conference paper to ICLR 2025
summary: It has been reported that LLMs can recognize their own writing. As this has potential implications for AI safety, yet is relatively understudied, we investigate the phenomenon, seeking to establish whether it robustly occurs at the behavioral level, how the observed behavior is achieved, and whether it can be controlled. First, we find that the Llama3-8b-Instruct chat model - but not the base Llama3-8b model - can reliably distinguish its own outputs from those of humans, and present evidence that the chat model is likely using its experience with its own outputs, acquired during post-training, to succeed at the writing recognition task. Second, we identify a vector in the residual stream of the model that is differentially activated when the model makes a correct self-written-text recognition judgment, show that the vector activates in response to information relevant to self-authorship, present evidence that the vector is related to the concept of "self" in the model, and demonstrate that the vector is causally related to the model's ability to perceive and assert self-authorship. Finally, we show that the vector can be used to control both the model's behavior and its perception, steering the model to claim or disclaim authorship by applying the vector to the model's output as it generates it, and steering the model to believe or disbelieve it wrote arbitrary texts by applying the vector to them as the model reads them.

AI.tldr: LLMs (specifically Llama3-8b-Instruct) can recognize their own writing, with evidence pointing to post-training experience enabling this capability. Researchers identified a neural vector causally linked to self-authorship perception and demonstrated control over both the model's authorship claims and beliefs through manipulation of this vector.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the potential AI safety implications of LLMs being able to recognize their own writing, which is an understudied phenomenon with significant implications for understanding AI self-awareness and potential control mechanisms.
AI.method: The study used the Llama3-8b-Instruct model (comparing it to the base Llama3-8b model) for writing recognition tasks. Researchers analyzed the model's residual stream to identify a specific vector activated during correct self-written-text recognition. They performed causal intervention experiments to demonstrate the vector's role in self-authorship perception and used this vector to control the model's behavior and perceptions.
AI.result: 1) Llama3-8b-Instruct reliably distinguishes its own outputs from human outputs, while the base model cannot. 2) This capability likely comes from post-training experience. 3) A specific neural vector was identified that differentially activates during correct self-recognition. 4) This vector is causally related to self-authorship perception. 5) The vector can be used to control both the model's behavior (making it claim/disclaim authorship) and its perceptions (making it believe/disbelieve it wrote arbitrary texts).
AI.conclusion: LLMs can develop the ability to recognize their own writing through post-training, with this capability localized to specific neural representations that can be manipulated to control both the model's authorship claims and its beliefs about authorship. This has significant implications for AI safety and understanding of AI self-awareness.

title: Circuit Complexity of Hierarchical Knowledge Tracing and Implications for Log-Precision Transformers
summary: Knowledge tracing models mastery over interconnected concepts, often organized by prerequisites. We analyze hierarchical prerequisite propagation through a circuit-complexity lens to clarify what is provable about transformer-style computation on deep concept hierarchies. Using recent results that log-precision transformers lie in logspace-uniform $\mathsf{TC}^0$, we formalize prerequisite-tree tasks including recursive-majority mastery propagation. Unconditionally, recursive-majority propagation lies in $\mathsf{NC}^1$ via $O(\log n)$-depth bounded-fanin circuits, while separating it from uniform $\mathsf{TC}^0$ would require major progress on open lower bounds. Under a monotonicity restriction, we obtain an unconditional barrier: alternating ALL/ANY prerequisite trees yield a strict depth hierarchy for \emph{monotone} threshold circuits. Empirically, transformer encoders trained on recursive-majority trees converge to permutation-invariant shortcuts; explicit structure alone does not prevent this, but auxiliary supervision on intermediate subtrees elicits structure-dependent computation and achieves near-perfect accuracy at depths 3--4. These findings motivate structure-aware objectives and iterative mechanisms for prerequisite-sensitive knowledge tracing on deep hierarchies.

AI.tldr: Transformers struggle with deep hierarchical prerequisite structures in knowledge tracing, converging to shortcuts instead of hierarchical reasoning, but intermediate supervision helps.
AI.motivation: Knowledge tracing requires modeling mastery over interconnected concepts with prerequisite relationships, but it's unclear what transformer-style models can actually compute on deep concept hierarchies, especially regarding hierarchical prerequisite propagation.
AI.method: Analyzed hierarchical prerequisite propagation using circuit-complexity lens, formalizing prerequisite-tree tasks like recursive-majority mastery propagation. Used theoretical analysis of log-precision transformers in logspace-uniform TC⁰ and empirical study with transformer encoders on recursive-majority trees with/without auxiliary supervision.
AI.result: Theoretically, recursive-majority propagation lies in NC¹, separating it from uniform TC⁰ would require major progress. Monotonically alternating ALL/ANY trees yield strict depth hierarchy for monotone threshold circuits. Empirically, transformers converge to permutation-invariant shortcuts without structure but achieve near-perfect accuracy at depths 3-4 with auxiliary supervision on intermediate subtrees.
AI.conclusion: Explicit structure alone doesn't prevent transformers from using shortcuts; structure-aware objectives and iterative mechanisms are needed for prerequisite-sensitive knowledge tracing on deep hierarchies.

title: Mitigating Many-Shot Jailbreaking
summary: Many-shot jailbreaking (MSJ) is an adversarial technique that exploits the long context windows of modern LLMs to circumvent model safety training by including in the prompt many examples of a "fake" assistant responding inappropriately before the final request. With enough examples, the model's in-context learning abilities override its safety training, and it responds as if it were the "fake" assistant. In this work, we probe the effectiveness of different fine-tuning and input sanitization approaches on mitigating MSJ attacks, alone and in combination. We find incremental mitigation effectiveness for each, and show that the combined techniques significantly reduce the effectiveness of MSJ attacks, while retaining model performance in benign in-context learning and conversational tasks. We suggest that our approach could meaningfully ameliorate this vulnerability if incorporated into model safety post-training.

AI.tldr: Investigation of fine-tuning and input sanitization techniques to mitigate Many-shot Jailbreaking attacks on LLMs
AI.motivation: Many-shot jailbreaking (MSJ) exploits long context windows to circumvent LLM safety training through adversarial examples, posing a security vulnerability that needs addressing
AI.method: Probing effectiveness of different fine-tuning and input sanitization approaches, alone and in combination, to mitigate MSJ attacks
AI.result: Incremental mitigation effectiveness for each technique individually, and significant reduction of MSJ effectiveness when combined while retaining model performance in benign tasks
AI.conclusion: Combined fine-tuning and input sanitization approach could meaningfully ameliorate MSJ vulnerability if incorporated into model safety post-training

title: Unveiling Hidden Convexity in Deep Learning: a Sparse Signal Processing Perspective
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs), particularly those using Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) activation functions, have achieved remarkable success across diverse machine learning tasks, including image recognition, audio processing, and language modeling. Despite this success, the non-convex nature of DNN loss functions complicates optimization and limits theoretical understanding. In this paper, we highlight how recently developed convex equivalences of ReLU NNs and their connections to sparse signal processing models can address the challenges of training and understanding NNs. Recent research has uncovered several hidden convexities in the loss landscapes of certain NN architectures, notably two-layer ReLU networks and other deeper or varied architectures. This paper seeks to provide an accessible and educational overview that bridges recent advances in the mathematics of deep learning with traditional signal processing, encouraging broader signal processing applications.

AI.tldr: Recent research reveals hidden convexities in ReLU neural network loss landscapes, connecting neural networks with traditional sparse signal processing models to improve training and understanding.
AI.motivation: Despite the remarkable success of DNNs (especially with ReLU activations) in various ML tasks, the non-convex nature of their loss functions complicates optimization and limits theoretical understanding. There's a need to bridge advances in deep learning mathematics with traditional signal processing.
AI.method: Leverages recently discovered convex equivalences of ReLU neural networks and their connections to sparse signal processing models. Focuses on examining hidden convexities in loss landscapes of certain NN architectures, particularly two-layer ReLU networks and other deeper/varied architectures.
AI.result: The paper provides an accessible overview demonstrating how convex equivalences and sparse signal processing models can address training challenges and improve theoretical understanding of neural networks.
AI.conclusion: By bridging recent mathematical advances in deep learning with traditional signal processing, the paper encourages broader signal processing applications and offers promising approaches to overcome optimization challenges in neural network training.

title: Evidence for Limited Metacognition in LLMs
summary: The possibility of LLM self-awareness and even sentience is gaining increasing public attention and has major safety and policy implications, but the science of measuring them is still in a nascent state. Here we introduce a novel methodology for quantitatively evaluating metacognitive abilities in LLMs. Taking inspiration from research on metacognition in nonhuman animals, our approach eschews model self-reports and instead tests to what degree models can strategically deploy knowledge of internal states. Using two experimental paradigms, we demonstrate that frontier LLMs introduced since early 2024 show increasingly strong evidence of certain metacognitive abilities, specifically the ability to assess and utilize their own confidence in their ability to answer factual and reasoning questions correctly and the ability to anticipate what answers they would give and utilize that information appropriately. We buttress these behavioral findings with an analysis of the token probabilities returned by the models, which suggests the presence of an upstream internal signal that could provide the basis for metacognition. We further find that these abilities 1) are limited in resolution, 2) emerge in context-dependent manners, and 3) seem to be qualitatively different from those of humans. We also report intriguing differences across models of similar capabilities, suggesting that LLM post-training may have a role in developing metacognitive abilities.

AI.tldr: LLMs show increasing metacognitive abilities since early 2024, including assessing confidence in answers and anticipating responses, but these abilities differ from humans and are context-dependent.
AI.motivation: Public attention to LLM self-awareness and sentience raises safety concerns, but scientific measurement of metacognition in LLMs remains underdeveloped, creating a need for quantitative evaluation methods beyond self-reports.
AI.method: Inspired by animal metacognition research, the approach tests strategic deployment of internal state knowledge. Two experimental paradigms measure: 1) ability to assess and utilize confidence in factual/reasoning answers, and 2) ability to anticipate and appropriately use expected answers. Token probability analysis examines internal signals.
AI.result: Frontier LLMs since early 2024 show increasing metacognitive abilities. These abilities are: 1) limited in resolution, 2) context-dependent, and 3) qualitatively different from humans. Token analysis suggests upstream internal signals. Model differences indicate post-training may develop metacognition.
AI.conclusion: LLMs demonstrate measurable metacognitive abilities, though different from humans, providing a scientific foundation for assessing LLM self-awareness and addressing safety implications. Post-training appears to enhance these abilities.

title: Symbolic--KAN: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks with Discrete Symbolic Structure for Interpretable Learning
summary: Symbolic discovery of governing equations is a long-standing goal in scientific machine learning, yet a fundamental trade-off persists between interpretability and scalable learning. Classical symbolic regression methods yield explicit analytic expressions but rely on combinatorial search, whereas neural networks scale efficiently with data and dimensionality but produce opaque representations. In this work, we introduce Symbolic Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (Symbolic-KANs), a neural architecture that bridges this gap by embedding discrete symbolic structure directly within a trainable deep network. Symbolic-KANs represent multivariate functions as compositions of learned univariate primitives applied to learned scalar projections, guided by a library of analytic primitives, hierarchical gating, and symbolic regularization that progressively sharpens continuous mixtures into one-hot selections. After gated training and discretization, each active unit selects a single primitive and projection direction, yielding compact closed-form expressions without post-hoc symbolic fitting. Symbolic-KANs further act as scalable primitive discovery mechanisms, identifying the most relevant analytic components that can subsequently inform candidate libraries for sparse equation-learning methods. We demonstrate that Symbolic-KAN reliably recovers correct primitive terms and governing structures in data-driven regression and inverse dynamical systems. Moreover, the framework extends to forward and inverse physics-informed learning of partial differential equations, producing accurate solutions directly from governing constraints while constructing compact symbolic representations whose selected primitives reflect the true analytical structure of the underlying equations. These results position Symbolic-KAN as a step toward scalable, interpretable, and mechanistically grounded learning of governing laws.

AI.tldr: Symbolic-KAN bridges neural network scalability with symbolic regression interpretability by embedding discrete symbolic structure within trainable deep networks, enabling automatic discovery of compact closed-form governing equations.
AI.motivation: There's a fundamental trade-off between interpretability (from classical symbolic regression) and scalable learning (from neural networks). Classical methods provide explicit analytic expressions but rely on inefficient combinatorial search, while neural networks scale well but produce opaque representations that lack interpretability.
AI.method: Introduces Symbolic Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (Symbolic-KANs) that embed discrete symbolic structure in trainable deep networks. They represent multivariate functions as compositions of learned univariate primitives applied to learned scalar projections, using a library of analytic primitives, hierarchical gating, and symbolic regularization that progressively sharpens continuous mixtures into one-hot selections.
AI.result: Symbolic-KAN reliably recovers correct primitive terms and governing structures in data-driven regression and inverse dynamical systems. It extends to forward and inverse physics-informed learning of PDEs, producing accurate solutions while constructing compact symbolic representations whose selected primitives reflect the true analytical structure of underlying equations.
AI.conclusion: Symbolic-KAN represents a step toward scalable, interpretable, and mechanistically grounded learning of governing laws by bridging the gap between neural network efficiency and symbolic regression interpretability.

title: Why the Maximum Second Derivative of Activations Matters for Adversarial Robustness
summary: This work investigates the critical role of activation function curvature -- quantified by the maximum second derivative $\max|σ''|$ -- in adversarial robustness. Using the Recursive Curvature-Tunable Activation Family (RCT-AF), which enables precise control over curvature through parameters $α$ and $β$, we systematically analyze this relationship. Our study reveals a fundamental trade-off: insufficient curvature limits model expressivity, while excessive curvature amplifies the normalized Hessian diagonal norm of the loss, leading to sharper minima that hinder robust generalization. This results in a non-monotonic relationship where optimal adversarial robustness consistently occurs when $\max|σ''|$ falls within 4 to 10, a finding that holds across diverse network architectures, datasets, and adversarial training methods. We provide theoretical insights into how activation curvature affects the diagonal elements of the hessian matrix of the loss, and experimentally demonstrate that the normalized Hessian diagonal norm exhibits a U-shaped dependence on $\max|σ''|$, with its minimum within the optimal robustness range, thereby validating the proposed mechanism.

AI.tldr: Optimal adversarial robustness requires moderate activation function curvature (max|σ''| between 4-10), balancing expressivity and loss landscape sharpness.
AI.motivation: Understand how activation function curvature affects adversarial robustness, as there's a gap in systematic analysis of this relationship despite known importance of activation functions in neural networks.
AI.method: Use RCT-AF (Recursive Curvature-Tunable Activation Family) with parameters α and β to precisely control curvature, analyze across diverse network architectures, datasets, and adversarial training methods.
AI.result: Found non-monotonic relationship: optimal adversarial robustness consistently occurs when max|σ''| falls within 4 to 10, with normalized Hessian diagonal norm showing U-shaped dependence on curvature.
AI.conclusion: Moderate activation curvature is crucial for adversarial robustness - insufficient curvature limits expressivity while excessive curvature creates sharper minima that hurt robust generalization.

title: An Invariant Compiler for Neural ODEs in AI-Accelerated Scientific Simulation
summary: Neural ODEs are increasingly used as continuous-time models for scientific and sensor data, but unconstrained neural ODEs can drift and violate domain invariants (e.g., conservation laws), yielding physically implausible solutions. In turn, this can compound error in long-horizon prediction and surrogate simulation. Existing solutions typically aim to enforce invariance by soft penalties or other forms of regularization, which can reduce overall error but do not guarantee that trajectories will not leave the constraint manifold. We introduce the invariant compiler, a framework that enforces invariants by construction: it treats invariants as first-class types and uses an LLM-driven compilation workflow to translate a generic neural ODE specification into a structure-preserving architecture whose trajectories remain on the admissible manifold in continuous time (and up to numerical integration error in practice). This compiler view cleanly separates what must be preserved (scientific structure) from what is learned from data (dynamics within that structure). It provides a systematic design pattern for invariant-respecting neural surrogates across scientific domains.

AI.tldr: A compiler framework that enforces domain invariants in neural ODEs by construction, using LLM-driven compilation to translate generic neural ODE specs into structure-preserving architectures that respect physical constraints.
AI.motivation: Unconstrained neural ODEs can drift and violate important domain invariants like conservation laws, leading to physically implausible solutions that compound errors in long-horizon predictions and surrogate simulations. Existing regularization methods don't guarantee trajectories stay on constraint manifolds.
AI.method: The "invariant compiler" treats invariants as first-class types and uses an LLM-driven compilation workflow to translate generic neural ODE specifications into structure-preserving architectures. It separates scientific structure preservation from learned dynamics.
AI.result: The framework produces neural ODE architectures whose trajectories remain on the admissible manifold in continuous time (within numerical integration error), providing guaranteed invariance enforcement rather than just regularization.
AI.conclusion: This compiler approach provides a systematic design pattern for creating invariant-respecting neural surrogates across scientific domains, cleanly separating what must be preserved (scientific structure) from what is learned from data (dynamics within that structure).

title: Deep Convolutional Neural Networks for predicting highest priority functional group in organic molecules
summary: Our work addresses the problem of predicting the highest priority functional group present in an organic molecule. Functional Groups are groups of bound atoms that determine the physical and chemical properties of organic molecules. In the presence of multiple functional groups, the dominant functional group determines the compound's properties. Fourier-transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) is a commonly used spectroscopic method for identifying the presence or absence of functional groups within a compound. We propose the use of a Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) to predict the highest priority functional group from the Fourier-transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) of the organic molecule. We have compared our model with other previously applied Machine Learning (ML) method Support Vector Machine (SVM) and reasoned why CNN outperforms it.

AI.tldr: Deep CNN predicts dominant functional groups in organic molecules from FTIR spectra, outperforming SVM methods.
AI.motivation: Functional groups determine organic molecule properties, and FTIR spectroscopy is commonly used to identify them, but traditional methods need improvement for predicting the highest priority functional group when multiple groups are present.
AI.method: Proposed using Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) to analyze Fourier-transform infrared spectra (FTIR) and predict the dominant functional group, comparing performance with Support Vector Machine (SVM).
AI.result: CNN model outperformed SVM in predicting the highest priority functional group from FTIR spectra.
AI.conclusion: Deep learning approaches, specifically CNNs, are superior to traditional ML methods like SVM for functional group prediction from spectroscopic data, potentially improving chemical analysis and characterization.

title: Can VLMs Reason Robustly? A Neuro-Symbolic Investigation
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have been applied to a wide range of reasoning tasks, yet it remains unclear whether they can reason robustly under distribution shifts. In this paper, we study covariate shifts in which the perceptual input distribution changes while the underlying prediction rules do not. To investigate this question, we consider visual deductive reasoning tasks, where a model is required to answer a query given an image and logical rules defined over the object concepts in the image. Empirically, we find that VLMs fine-tuned through gradient-based end-to-end training can achieve high in-distribution accuracy but fail to generalize under such shifts, suggesting that fine-tuning does not reliably induce the underlying reasoning function. This motivates a neuro-symbolic perspective that decouples perception from reasoning. However, we further observe that recent neuro-symbolic approaches that rely on black-box components for reasoning can still exhibit inconsistent robustness across tasks. To address this issue, we propose VLC, a neuro-symbolic method that combines VLM-based concept recognition with circuit-based symbolic reasoning. In particular, task rules are compiled into a symbolic program, specifically a circuit, which executes the rules exactly over the object concepts recognized by the VLM. Experiments on three visual deductive reasoning tasks with distinct rule sets show that VLC consistently achieves strong performance under covariate shifts, highlighting its ability to support robust reasoning.

AI.tldr: VLC is a neuro-symbolic method that combines VLM-based perception with circuit-based symbolic reasoning to achieve robust generalization under covariate shifts in visual deductive reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) fail to generalize robustly under covariate shifts (perceptual distribution changes) in visual deductive reasoning tasks, and even recent neuro-symbolic approaches with black-box reasoning components show inconsistent robustness across tasks.
AI.method: VLC (VLM-based concept recognition with circuit-based symbolic reasoning) decouples perception from reasoning by having VLMs handle concept recognition while task rules are compiled into symbolic programs (circuits) that execute rules exactly over recognized concepts.
AI.result: Experiments on three visual deductive reasoning tasks with distinct rule sets show VLC consistently achieves strong performance under covariate shifts, demonstrating robust reasoning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The proposed VLC approach effectively addresses the robustness limitations of fine-tuned VLMs and black-box neuro-symbolic methods by combining neural perception with exact symbolic reasoning, enabling reliable generalization under distribution shifts.

title: HDPO: Hybrid Distillation Policy Optimization via Privileged Self-Distillation
summary: Large language models trained with reinforcement learning (RL) for mathematical reasoning face a fundamental challenge: on problems the model cannot solve at all - "cliff" prompts - the RL gradient vanishes entirely, preventing any learning signal from reaching these failure modes. We introduce Hybrid Distillation Policy Optimization (HDPO), which augments standard RL with privileged self-distillation targeting cliff prompts. On each training step, HDPO identifies prompts where all rollouts fail, generates privileged rollouts by providing the model with ground-truth information, filters for correct solutions, and distills the teacher's token-level distribution into the student. Because teacher and student share the same weights - differing only in their input - the realizability gap is provably bounded, unlike cross-model distillation. We prove that R=1 filtered privileged generation recovers the optimal KL-regularized RL policy in the hard-threshold limit. Experiments on OpenMathInstruct-2 with Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B-Instruct show that HDPO consistently improves coverage metrics (pass@4 by +0.8-1.1%, pass@8 by +0.4-1.7%) while maintaining greedy accuracy, with the distillation weight lambda providing direct control over the exploration-exploitation tradeoff.

AI.tldr: HDPO (Hybrid Distillation Policy Optimization) addresses vanishing gradients in RL-trained LLMs for math reasoning by combining standard RL with privileged self-distillation on "cliff" prompts (unsolvable problems), improving coverage without sacrificing greedy accuracy.
AI.motivation: Large language models trained with RL for mathematical reasoning face vanishing gradients on "cliff prompts" - problems the model cannot solve at all. This prevents any learning signal from reaching these failure modes, creating a fundamental training challenge.
AI.method: HDPO augments standard RL with privileged self-distillation targeting cliff prompts. It identifies prompts where all rollouts fail, generates privileged rollouts by providing the model with ground-truth information, filters for correct solutions, and distills the teacher's token-level distribution into the student. The teacher and student share weights but differ in input, bounding the realizability gap.
AI.result: Experiments on OpenMathInstruct-2 with Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B-Instruct show HDPO improves coverage metrics (pass@4 by +0.8-1.1%, pass@8 by +0.4-1.7%) while maintaining greedy accuracy. The distillation weight λ provides direct control over exploration-exploitation tradeoff.
AI.conclusion: HDPO effectively addresses the cliff prompt problem in RL-trained math reasoning models through privileged self-distillation, offering theoretical guarantees and practical improvements in coverage while maintaining accuracy, with tunable exploration-exploitation balance.

title: The Luna Bound Propagator for Formal Analysis of Neural Networks
summary: The parameterized CROWN analysis, a.k.a., alpha-CROWN, has emerged as a practically successful bound propagation method for neural network verification. However, existing implementations of alpha-CROWN are limited to Python, which complicates integration into existing DNN verifiers and long-term production-level systems. We introduce Luna, a new bound propagator implemented in C++. Luna supports Interval Bound Propagation, the CROWN analysis, and the alpha-CROWN analysis over a general computational graph. We describe the architecture of Luna and show that it is competitive with the state-of-the-art alpha-CROWN implementation in terms of both bound tightness and computational efficiency on benchmarks from VNN-COMP 2025.

AI.tldr: Luna is a new C++ implementation of the alpha-CROWN bound propagation method for neural network verification, offering competitive performance with existing Python implementations while being more suitable for integration into production systems.
AI.motivation: Existing alpha-CROWN implementations are limited to Python, which complicates integration into existing DNN verifiers and long-term production-level systems. There's a need for a more portable, efficient implementation that can be easily integrated into verification frameworks.
AI.method: Developed Luna, a new bound propagator implemented in C++ that supports Interval Bound Propagation, CROWN analysis, and alpha-CROWN analysis over a general computational graph. The paper describes the architecture of Luna and benchmarks it against existing implementations.
AI.result: Luna is competitive with state-of-the-art alpha-CROWN implementations in terms of both bound tightness and computational efficiency on benchmarks from VNN-COMP 2025.
AI.conclusion: Luna provides a practical C++ implementation of alpha-CROWN bound propagation that addresses the limitations of Python-based implementations while maintaining competitive performance, making it suitable for integration into production-level neural network verification systems.

title: Off-Policy Safe Reinforcement Learning with Constrained Optimistic Exploration
comment: 21 pages, 9 figures, accepted by ICLR 2026 poster
summary: When safety is formulated as a limit of cumulative cost, safe reinforcement learning (RL) aims to learn policies that maximize return subject to the cost constraint in data collection and deployment. Off-policy safe RL methods, although offering high sample efficiency, suffer from constraint violations due to cost-agnostic exploration and estimation bias in cumulative cost. To address this issue, we propose Constrained Optimistic eXploration Q-learning (COX-Q), an off-policy safe RL algorithm that integrates cost-bounded online exploration and conservative offline distributional value learning. First, we introduce a novel cost-constrained optimistic exploration strategy that resolves gradient conflicts between reward and cost in the action space and adaptively adjusts the trust region to control the training cost. Second, we adopt truncated quantile critics to stabilize the cost value learning. Quantile critics also quantify epistemic uncertainty to guide exploration. Experiments on safe velocity, safe navigation, and autonomous driving tasks demonstrate that COX-Q achieves high sample efficiency, competitive test safety performance, and controlled data collection cost. The results highlight COX-Q as a promising RL method for safety-critical applications.

AI.tldr: COX-Q is an off-policy safe RL algorithm that combines cost-bounded optimistic exploration with conservative distributional value learning to achieve high sample efficiency while controlling constraint violations during data collection.
AI.motivation: Off-policy safe RL methods suffer from constraint violations due to cost-agnostic exploration and estimation bias in cumulative cost, limiting their practical application in safety-critical domains.
AI.method: Proposes Constrained Optimistic eXploration Q-learning (COX-Q) with two key components: 1) cost-constrained optimistic exploration strategy that resolves reward-cost gradient conflicts and adaptively adjusts trust regions, 2) truncated quantile critics to stabilize cost value learning and quantify epistemic uncertainty for guided exploration.
AI.result: Experiments on safe velocity, safe navigation, and autonomous driving tasks show COX-Q achieves high sample efficiency, competitive test safety performance, and controlled data collection cost compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: COX-Q presents a promising RL approach for safety-critical applications by effectively balancing exploration efficiency with safety constraints through its novel integration of optimistic exploration and conservative distributional learning.

title: Optimal Variance-Dependent Regret Bounds for Infinite-Horizon MDPs
summary:
Online reinforcement learning in infinite-horizon Markov decision processes (MDPs) remains less theoretically and algorithmically developed than its episodic counterpart, with many algorithms suffering from high ``burn-in'' costs and failing to adapt to benign instance-specific complexity. In this work, we address these shortcomings for two infinite-horizon objectives: the classical average-reward regret and the $γ$-regret. We develop a single tractable UCB-style algorithm applicable to both settings, which achieves the first optimal variance-dependent regret guarantees. Our regret bounds in both settings take the form $\tilde{O}( \sqrt{SA\,\text{Var}} + \text{lower-order terms})$, where $S,A$ are the state and action space sizes, and $\text{Var}$ captures cumulative transition variance. This implies minimax-optimal average-reward and $γ$-regret bounds in the worst case but also adapts to easier problem instances, for example yielding nearly constant regret in deterministic MDPs. Furthermore, our algorithm enjoys significantly improved lower-order terms for the average-reward setting. With prior knowledge of the optimal bias span $\Vert h^\star\Vert_\text{sp}$, our algorithm obtains lower-order terms scaling as $\Vert h^\star\Vert_\text{sp} S^2 A$, which we prove is optimal in both $\Vert h^\star\Vert_\text{sp}$ and $A$.
  Without prior knowledge, we prove that no algorithm can have lower-order terms smaller than $\Vert h^\star \Vert_\text{sp}^2 S A$, and we provide a prior-free algorithm whose lower-order terms scale as $\Vert h^\star\Vert_\text{sp}^2 S^3 A$, nearly matching this lower bound. Taken together, these results completely characterize the optimal dependence on $\Vert h^\star\Vert_\text{sp}$ in both leading and lower-order terms, and reveal a fundamental gap in what is achievable with and without prior knowledge.

AI.tldr: A single UCB-style algorithm achieves optimal variance-dependent regret bounds for infinite-horizon MDPs in both average-reward and γ-regret settings, with adaptive performance to instance complexity and tight characterization of optimal bias span dependence.
AI.motivation: Existing online RL algorithms for infinite-horizon MDPs suffer from high burn-in costs and fail to adapt to instance-specific complexity. The episodic RL counterpart is better developed theoretically and algorithmically.
AI.method: Developed a tractable UCB-style algorithm applicable to both average-reward and γ-regret settings that achieves variance-dependent regret guarantees with adaptive performance to problem instance difficulty.
AI.result: Achieves regret bounds of form Õ(√(SA·Var) + lower-order terms) for both settings, optimal dependence on bias span ‖h*‖ₛₚ, with and without prior knowledge, and matches lower bounds on lower-order terms.
AI.conclusion: The work completely characterizes optimal dependence on bias span, reveals fundamental gap between what's achievable with vs without prior knowledge, and provides practical algorithms for infinite-horizon RL with instance-adaptive performance.

title: GRMLR: Knowledge-Enhanced Small-Data Learning for Deep-Sea Cold Seep Stage Inference
summary: Deep-sea cold seep stage assessment has traditionally relied on costly, high-risk manned submersible operations and visual surveys of macrofauna. Although microbial communities provide a promising and more cost-effective alternative, reliable inference remains challenging because the available deep-sea dataset is extremely small ($n = 13$) relative to the microbial feature dimension ($p = 26$), making purely data-driven models highly prone to overfitting. To address this, we propose a knowledge-enhanced classification framework that incorporates an ecological knowledge graph as a structural prior. By fusing macro-microbe coupling and microbial co-occurrence patterns, the framework internalizes established ecological logic into a \underline{\textbf{G}}raph-\underline{\textbf{R}}egularized \underline{\textbf{M}}ultinomial \underline{\textbf{L}}ogistic \underline{\textbf{R}}egression (GRMLR) model, effectively constraining the feature space through a manifold penalty to ensure biologically consistent classification. Importantly, the framework removes the need for macrofauna observations at inference time: macro-microbe associations are used only to guide training, whereas prediction relies solely on microbial abundance profiles. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms standard baselines, highlighting its potential as a robust and scalable framework for deep-sea ecological assessment.

AI.tldr: A knowledge-enhanced classification framework using ecological knowledge graphs as structural prior improves cold seep stage assessment from microbial data, overcoming overfitting in small deep-sea datasets.
AI.motivation: Traditional deep-sea cold seep stage assessment relies on costly manned submersibles and macrofauna surveys. Microbial communities offer a cost-effective alternative but face overfitting challenges due to extremely small datasets (n=13) relative to high-dimensional microbial features (p=26).
AI.method: Proposes a knowledge-enhanced classification framework incorporating ecological knowledge graph as structural prior. Uses macro-microbe coupling and microbial co-occurrence patterns to create a Graph-Regularized Multinomial Logistic Regression (GRMLR) model with manifold penalty, ensuring biologically consistent classification while only needing microbial data for inference.
AI.result: The approach significantly outperforms standard baselines, demonstrating robust performance despite the extreme data scarcity (13 samples, 26 features). The framework successfully removes the need for macrofauna observations during inference while using ecological knowledge to guide training.
AI.conclusion: The knowledge-enhanced framework shows potential as a robust, scalable solution for deep-sea ecological assessment by leveraging ecological knowledge graphs to overcome data scarcity challenges in microbial-based classification tasks.

title: Wireless communication empowers online scheduling of partially-observable transportation multi-robot systems in a smart factory
summary: Achieving agile and reconfigurable production flows in smart factories depends on online multi-robot task assignment (MRTA), which requires online collision-free and congestion-free route scheduling of transportation multi-robot systems (T-MRS), e.g., collaborative automatic guided vehicles (AGVs). Due to the real-time operational requirements and dynamic interactions between T-MRS and production MRS, online scheduling under partial observability in dynamic factory environments remains a significant and under-explored challenge. This paper proposes a novel communication-enabled online scheduling framework that explicitly couples wireless machine-to-machine (M2M) networking with route scheduling, enabling AGVs to exchange intention information, e.g., planned routes, to overcome partial observations and assist complex computation of online scheduling. Specifically, we determine intelligent AGVs' intention and sensor data as new M2M traffic and tailor the retransmission-free multi-link transmission networking to meet real-time operation demands. This scheduling-oriented networking is then integrated with a simulated annealing-based MRTA scheme and a congestion-aware A*-based route scheduling method. The integrated communication and scheduling scheme allows AGVs to dynamically adjust collision-free and congestion-free routes with reduced computational overhead. Numerical experiments shows the impacts from wireless communication on the performance of T-MRS and suggest that the proposed integrated scheme significantly enhances scheduling efficiency compared to other baselines, even under high AGV load conditions and limited channel resources. Moreover, the results reveal that the scheduling-oriented wireless M2M communication design fundamentally differs from human-to-human communications, implying new technological opportunities in a wireless networked smart factory.

AI.tldr: A communication-enabled online scheduling framework that integrates wireless M2M networking with route scheduling for AGVs in smart factories, improving scheduling efficiency under partial observability and dynamic conditions.
AI.motivation: Online multi-robot task assignment (MRTA) for smart factories requires real-time collision-free and congestion-free route scheduling, but current approaches struggle with partial observability in dynamic factory environments and lack integration between communication and scheduling systems.
AI.method: Proposes a novel framework that couples wireless M2M networking with route scheduling: 1) Uses AGV intention and sensor data as M2M traffic with retransmission-free multi-link transmission, 2) Integrates simulated annealing-based MRTA with congestion-aware A*-based route scheduling, 3) Enables AGVs to dynamically adjust routes through communication.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show the proposed integrated scheme significantly enhances scheduling efficiency compared to baselines, even under high AGV load and limited channel resources. The framework reduces computational overhead and reveals that scheduling-oriented M2M communication fundamentally differs from human-to-human communications.
AI.conclusion: The integration of scheduling-oriented wireless M2M communication with route scheduling enables more efficient online AGV scheduling in smart factories, overcoming partial observability challenges and opening new technological opportunities for wireless networked smart factories.

title: Kirchhoff-Inspired Neural Networks for Evolving High-Order Perception
summary: Deep learning architectures are fundamentally inspired by neuroscience, particularly the structure of the brain's sensory pathways, and have achieved remarkable success in learning informative data representations. Although these architectures mimic the communication mechanisms of biological neurons, their strategies for information encoding and transmission are fundamentally distinct. Biological systems depend on dynamic fluctuations in membrane potential; by contrast, conventional deep networks optimize weights and biases by adjusting the strengths of inter-neural connections, lacking a systematic mechanism to jointly characterize the interplay among signal intensity, coupling structure, and state evolution. To tackle this limitation, we propose the Kirchhoff-Inspired Neural Network (KINN), a state-variable-based network architecture constructed based on Kirchhoff's current law. KINN derives numerically stable state updates from fundamental ordinary differential equations, enabling the explicit decoupling and encoding of higher-order evolutionary components within a single layer while preserving physical consistency, interpretability, and end-to-end trainability. Extensive experiments on partial differential equation (PDE) solving and ImageNet image classification validate that KINN outperforms state-of-the-art existing methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces KINN (Kirchhoff-Inspired Neural Network), a state-variable-based architecture inspired by Kirchhoff's current law that addresses limitations in how conventional deep networks encode information compared to biological systems.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning architectures lack systematic mechanisms to jointly characterize the interplay among signal intensity, coupling structure, and state evolution, unlike biological systems which use dynamic membrane potential fluctuations. There's a gap between how biological neurons encode information and how conventional neural networks operate.
AI.method: The authors propose KINN, which constructs a network architecture based on Kirchhoff's current law using state-variable-based approach. It derives numerically stable state updates from ordinary differential equations, enabling explicit decoupling and encoding of higher-order evolutionary components within a single layer while maintaining physical consistency and interpretability.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on partial differential equation (PDE) solving and ImageNet image classification show that KINN outperforms state-of-the-art existing methods.
AI.conclusion: KINN successfully bridges the gap between biological information encoding principles and deep learning architectures by incorporating Kirchhoff-inspired principles, resulting in improved performance while preserving physical interpretability and end-to-end trainability.

title: Transcending Classical Neural Network Boundaries: A Quantum-Classical Synergistic Paradigm for Seismic Data Processing
summary: In recent years, a number of neural-network (NN) methods have exhibited good performance in seismic data processing, such as denoising, interpolation, and frequency-band extension. However, these methods rely on stacked perceptrons and standard activation functions, which imposes a bottleneck on the representational capacity of deep-learning models, making it difficult to capture the complex and non-stationary dynamics of seismic wavefields. Different from the classical perceptron-stacked NNs which are fundamentally confined to real-valued Euclidean spaces, the quantum NNs leverage the exponential state space of quantum mechanics to map the features into high-dimensional Hilbert spaces, transcending the representational boundary of classical NNs. Based on this insight, we propose a quantum-classical synergistic generative adversarial network (QC-GAN) for seismic data processing, serving as the first application of quantum NNs in seismic exploration. In QC-GAN, a quantum pathway is used to exploit the high-order feature correlations, while the convolutional pathway specializes in extracting the waveform structures of seismic wavefields. Furthermore, we design a QC feature complementarity loss to enforce the feature orthogonality in the proposed QC-GAN. This novel loss function can ensure that the two pathways encode non-overlapping information to enrich the capacity of feature representation. On the whole, by synergistically integrating the quantum and convolutional pathways, the proposed QC-GAN breaks the representational bottleneck inherent in classical GAN. Experimental results on denoising and interpolation tasks demonstrate that QC-GAN preserves wavefield continuity and amplitude-phase information under complex noise conditions.

AI.tldr: QC-GAN: A quantum-classical synergistic GAN for seismic data processing that integrates quantum NNs for high-order feature correlations with convolutional NNs for waveform extraction, overcoming representational limitations of classical NNs.
AI.motivation: Classical neural networks have limited representational capacity for complex seismic wavefields due to their Euclidean space constraints. Quantum NNs can map features into high-dimensional Hilbert spaces, offering superior representational power beyond classical approaches.
AI.method: Propose QC-GAN with two pathways: 1) quantum pathway for high-order feature correlations, and 2) convolutional pathway for extracting seismic waveform structures. A QC feature complementarity loss enforces feature orthogonality to ensure non-overlapping information encoding.
AI.result: Experimental results on denoising and interpolation tasks show that QC-GAN preserves wavefield continuity and amplitude-phase information under complex noise conditions, demonstrating improved performance over classical approaches.
AI.conclusion: QC-GAN successfully breaks the representational bottleneck of classical GANs by synergistically integrating quantum and convolutional pathways, marking the first application of quantum NNs in seismic exploration with promising results for complex seismic data processing.

title: Diet Your LLM: Dimension-wise Global Pruning of LLMs via Merging Task-specific Importance Score
comment: 14 pages, 10 figures. Code available at https://github.com/Jimmy145123/DIET
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities, but their massive scale poses significant challenges for practical deployment. Structured pruning offers a promising solution by removing entire dimensions or layers, yet existing methods face critical trade-offs: task-agnostic approaches cannot adapt to task-specific requirements, while task-aware methods require costly training to learn task adaptability. We propose DIET (Dimension-wise global pruning of LLMs via merging Task-wise importance scores), a training-free structured pruning method that combines dimension-level granularity with task-aware selection. DIET profiles activation magnitudes across tasks using only 100 samples per task, then applies majority voting to construct a single global mask. DIET does not require large costs from pre-computation or training. Experiments on seven zero-shot benchmarks using Gemma-2 2B and 9B models demonstrate the effectiveness of DIET; for example, at 20% sparsity on Gemma-2 2B, DIET achieves near 10% average accuracy improvement, compared to previous state-of-the-art structured pruning methods. This advantage persists across various sparsity levels and model scales, positioning DIET as a practical and robust choice for structured LLM pruning.

AI.tldr: DIET is a training-free structured pruning method for LLMs that uses task activation profiling with minimal data and majority voting to create global pruning masks, achieving significant performance improvements over existing methods.
AI.motivation: LLMs face deployment challenges due to massive scale, and existing structured pruning methods have critical trade-offs: task-agnostic approaches lack task adaptability while task-aware methods require costly training to learn task-specific requirements.
AI.method: DIET uses dimension-wise global pruning via merging task-wise importance scores. It profiles activation magnitudes across tasks using only 100 samples per task, then applies majority voting to construct a single global mask without requiring training or costly pre-computation.
AI.result: Experiments on seven zero-shot benchmarks using Gemma-2 2B and 9B models show DIET's effectiveness. At 20% sparsity on Gemma-2 2B, DIET achieves near 10% average accuracy improvement compared to previous state-of-the-art structured pruning methods, with advantages persisting across various sparsity levels and model scales.
AI.conclusion: DIET positions itself as a practical and robust choice for structured LLM pruning by combining dimension-level granularity with task-aware selection in a training-free approach that requires minimal data while delivering superior performance improvements.

title: Can we generate portable representations for clinical time series data using LLMs?
comment: Accepted to the 14th International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2026)
summary: Deploying clinical ML is slow and brittle: models that work at one hospital often degrade under distribution shifts at the next. In this work, we study a simple question -- can large language models (LLMs) create portable patient embeddings i.e. representations of patients enable a downstream predictor built on one hospital to be used elsewhere with minimal-to-no retraining and fine-tuning. To do so, we map from irregular ICU time series onto concise natural language summaries using a frozen LLM, then embed each summary with a frozen text embedding model to obtain a fixed length vector capable of serving as input to a variety of downstream predictors. Across three cohorts (MIMIC-IV, HIRID, PPICU), on multiple clinically grounded forecasting and classification tasks, we find that our approach is simple, easy to use and competitive with in-distribution with grid imputation, self-supervised representation learning, and time series foundation models, while exhibiting smaller relative performance drops when transferring to new hospitals. We study the variation in performance across prompt design, with structured prompts being crucial to reducing the variance of the predictive models without altering mean accuracy. We find that using these portable representations improves few-shot learning and does not increase demographic recoverability of age or sex relative to baselines, suggesting little additional privacy risk. Our work points to the potential that LLMs hold as tools to enable the scalable deployment of production grade predictive models by reducing the engineering overhead.

AI.tldr: LLMs can create portable patient embeddings from irregular ICU time series via natural language summaries, enabling downstream predictors to transfer across hospitals with minimal retraining while maintaining competitive performance.
AI.motivation: Clinical ML deployment is slow and brittle, with models often degrading under distribution shifts between hospitals. The paper aims to create portable patient embeddings that enable downstream predictors to work across different hospitals with minimal retraining.
AI.method: Map irregular ICU time series onto concise natural language summaries using a frozen LLM, then embed each summary with a frozen text embedding model to obtain fixed-length vectors. These vectors serve as inputs to downstream predictors, with careful prompt design (structured prompts) to reduce variance.
AI.result: The approach is competitive with in-distribution baselines (grid imputation, self-supervised representation learning, time series foundation models) while exhibiting smaller performance drops when transferring to new hospitals. Structured prompts reduce variance without altering mean accuracy. The method improves few-shot learning and doesn't increase demographic recoverability, suggesting minimal privacy risk.
AI.conclusion: LLMs show potential as tools for scalable deployment of production-grade predictive models by creating portable patient embeddings that reduce engineering overhead and enable better cross-hospital transfer.

title: Understanding the Challenges in Iterative Generative Optimization with LLMs
summary: Generative optimization uses large language models (LLMs) to iteratively improve artifacts (such as code, workflows or prompts) using execution feedback. It is a promising approach to building self-improving agents, yet in practice remains brittle: despite active research, only 9% of surveyed agents used any automated optimization. We argue that this brittleness arises because, to set up a learning loop, an engineer must make ``hidden'' design choices: What can the optimizer edit and what is the "right" learning evidence to provide at each update? We investigate three factors that affect most applications: the starting artifact, the credit horizon for execution traces, and batching trials and errors into learning evidence. Through case studies in MLAgentBench, Atari, and BigBench Extra Hard, we find that these design decisions can determine whether generative optimization succeeds, yet they are rarely made explicit in prior work. Different starting artifacts determine which solutions are reachable in MLAgentBench, truncated traces can still improve Atari agents, and larger minibatches do not monotonically improve generalization on BBEH. We conclude that the lack of a simple, universal way to set up learning loops across domains is a major hurdle for productionization and adoption. We provide practical guidance for making these choices.

AI.tldr: Generative optimization using LLMs is brittle in practice due to hidden design choices, and three key factors (starting artifact, credit horizon, batching) critically affect success.
AI.motivation: Despite being a promising approach for building self-improving agents, generative optimization remains brittle in practice, with only 9% of surveyed agents using automated optimization. The brittleness stems from hidden design choices engineers must make when setting up learning loops.
AI.method: Investigates three factors affecting most applications: starting artifact, credit horizon for execution traces, and batching trials and errors into learning evidence. Uses case studies in MLAgentBench, Atari, and BigBench Extra Hard (BBEH) to analyze these design decisions.
AI.result: Design decisions critically determine whether generative optimization succeeds: different starting artifacts affect solution reachability in MLAgentBench, truncated traces can still improve Atari agents, and larger minibatches don't monotonically improve generalization on BBEH.
AI.conclusion: The lack of a simple, universal way to set up learning loops across domains is a major hurdle for productionization and adoption. The paper provides practical guidance for making these hidden design choices explicit.

title: Stochastic Dimension-Free Zeroth-Order Estimator for High-Dimensional and High-Order PINNs
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for high-dimensional and high-order partial differential equations (PDEs) are primarily constrained by the $\mathcal{O}(d^k)$ spatial derivative complexity and the $\mathcal{O}(P)$ memory overhead of backpropagation (BP). While randomized spatial estimators successfully reduce the spatial complexity to $\mathcal{O}(1)$, their reliance on first-order optimization still leads to prohibitive memory consumption at scale. Zeroth-order (ZO) optimization offers a BP-free alternative; however, naively combining randomized spatial operators with ZO perturbations triggers a variance explosion of $\mathcal{O}(1/\varepsilon^2)$, leading to numerical divergence. To address these challenges, we propose the \textbf{S}tochastic \textbf{D}imension-free \textbf{Z}eroth-order \textbf{E}stimator (\textbf{SDZE}), a unified framework that achieves dimension-independent complexity in both space and memory. Specifically, SDZE leverages \emph{Common Random Numbers Synchronization (CRNS)} to algebraically cancel the $\mathcal{O}(1/\varepsilon^2)$ variance by locking spatial random seeds across perturbations. Furthermore, an \emph{implicit matrix-free subspace projection} is introduced to reduce parameter exploration variance from $\mathcal{O}(P)$ to $\mathcal{O}(r)$ while maintaining an $\mathcal{O}(1)$ optimizer memory footprint. Empirical results demonstrate that SDZE enables the training of 10-million-dimensional PINNs on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU, delivering significant improvements in speed and memory efficiency over state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: SDZE is a stochastic dimension-free zeroth-order estimator framework for training Physics-Informed Neural Networks on high-dimensional PDEs, achieving O(1) spatial complexity and O(1) memory overhead while avoiding the O(1/ε²) variance explosion that plagues naive zeroth-order methods.
AI.motivation: Current PINN training faces two major bottlenecks: (1) O(d^k) spatial derivative computational complexity for high-dimensional, high-order PDEs, and (2) O(P) memory overhead from backpropagation. While randomized spatial estimators solve the spatial complexity issue, they still require first-order optimization with prohibitive memory consumption. Zeroth-order optimization offers a BP-free alternative but suffers from O(1/ε²) variance explosion when combined with randomized spatial operators.
AI.method: SDZE combines two key innovations: 1) Common Random Numbers Synchronization (CRNS) that locks spatial random seeds across zeroth-order perturbations to algebraically cancel the O(1/ε²) variance, and 2) implicit matrix-free subspace projection that reduces parameter exploration variance from O(P) to O(r) while maintaining O(1) optimizer memory footprint.
AI.result: Empirical results show SDZE enables training of 10-million-dimensional PINNs on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU, delivering significant improvements in both speed and memory efficiency compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: SDZE provides a unified framework that achieves dimension-independent complexity in both spatial computation and memory usage for PINN training, effectively solving the variance explosion problem in zeroth-order optimization while maintaining computational efficiency for high-dimensional PDE problems.

title: i-IF-Learn: Iterative Feature Selection and Unsupervised Learning for High-Dimensional Complex Data
comment: 28 pages, 5 figures, including appendix. Accepted at AISTATS
summary: Unsupervised learning of high-dimensional data is challenging due to irrelevant or noisy features obscuring underlying structures. It's common that only a few features, called the influential features, meaningfully define the clusters. Recovering these influential features is helpful in data interpretation and clustering. We propose i-IF-Learn, an iterative unsupervised framework that jointly performs feature selection and clustering. Our core innovation is an adaptive feature selection statistic that effectively combines pseudo-label supervision with unsupervised signals, dynamically adjusting based on intermediate label reliability to mitigate error propagation common in iterative frameworks. Leveraging low-dimensional embeddings (PCA or Laplacian eigenmaps) followed by $k$-means, i-IF-Learn simultaneously outputs influential feature subset and clustering labels. Numerical experiments on gene microarray and single-cell RNA-seq datasets show that i-IF-Learn significantly surpasses classical and deep clustering baselines. Furthermore, using our selected influential features as preprocessing substantially enhances downstream deep models such as DeepCluster, UMAP, and VAE, highlighting the importance and effectiveness of targeted feature selection.

AI.tldr: i-IF-Learn: iterative unsupervised framework that jointly performs feature selection and clustering to identify influential features while mitigating error propagation in iterative methods.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised learning of high-dimensional data is challenging due to irrelevant/noisy features obscuring structures; only a few influential features meaningfully define clusters, and recovering them helps data interpretation and clustering.
AI.method: Iterative framework combining feature selection and clustering with adaptive feature selection statistic that combines pseudo-label supervision with unsupervised signals, using low-dimensional embeddings (PCA/Laplacian eigenmaps) followed by k-means.
AI.result: Significantly surpasses classical and deep clustering baselines on gene microarray and single-cell RNA-seq datasets; using selected features as preprocessing enhances downstream deep models like DeepCluster, UMAP, and VAE.
AI.conclusion: i-IF-Learn effectively identifies influential features while improving clustering performance, demonstrating the importance of targeted feature selection for unsupervised learning in high-dimensional data.

title: Lagrangian Relaxation Score-based Generation for Mixed Integer linear Programming
summary: Predict-and-search (PaS) methods have shown promise for accelerating mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) solving. However, existing approaches typically assume variable independence and rely on deterministic single-point predictions, which limits solution diversityand often necessitates extensive downstream search for high-quality solutions. In this paper, we propose \textbf{SRG}, a generative framework based on Lagrangian relaxation-guided stochastic differential equations (SDEs), with theoretical guarantees on solution quality. SRG leverages convolutional kernels to capture inter-variable dependencies while integrating Lagrangian relaxation to guide the sampling process toward feasible and near-optimal regions. Rather than producing a single estimate, SRG generates diverse, high-quality solution candidates that collectively define compact and effective trust-region subproblems for standard MILP solvers. Across multiple public benchmarks, SRG consistently outperforms existing machine learning baselines in solution quality. Moreover, SRG demonstrates strong zero-shot transferability: on unseen cross-scale/problem instances, it achieves competitive optimality with state-of-the-art exact solvers while significantly reducing computational overhead through faster search and superior solution quality.

AI.tldr: SRG is a generative framework using Lagrangian relaxation-guided SDEs to produce diverse, high-quality MILP solutions, outperforming existing ML methods and showing strong zero-shot transferability.
AI.motivation: Existing predict-and-search methods for MILP acceleration assume variable independence and rely on deterministic single-point predictions, which limits solution diversity and requires extensive downstream search for high-quality solutions.
AI.method: SRG uses Lagrangian relaxation-guided stochastic differential equations with convolutional kernels to capture inter-variable dependencies. It generates diverse solution candidates that define compact trust-region subproblems for standard MILP solvers.
AI.result: SRG consistently outperforms existing ML baselines in solution quality across multiple benchmarks and demonstrates strong zero-shot transferability, achieving competitive optimality with state-of-the-art solvers while reducing computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: SRG provides a theoretically-guaranteed generative framework for MILP solving that addresses limitations of existing approaches through diversity generation and efficient search, showing promising performance and transferability.

title: MoE-Sieve: Routing-Guided LoRA for Efficient MoE Fine-Tuning
summary: Standard LoRA fine-tuning of Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models applies adapters to every expert, yet our profiling shows that per-layer expert routing is highly skewed: a small subset of experts handles most tokens in each layer, while many others are rarely activated ("cold"). We propose MoE-Sieve, a simple routing-guided framework for LoRA fine-tuning, and pair it with a systematic profiling study of expert routing across architectures and tasks. The method is simple: profile routing counts on a small calibration set, select the top-k most-routed experts per layer, and apply LoRA only to those experts. Across two architecturally distinct MoE models and three diverse tasks, tuning only the top 25% routed experts per layer remains competitive with full LoRA, with mean differences within +/-1 percentage point across all conditions. This reduces LoRA trainable parameters by 70-73%, adapter checkpoint size by 71-73%, and wall-clock training time by up to 50%. We also observe a non-monotonic relationship between expert count and seed-to-seed variance, consistent with the hypothesis that adapting cold experts can introduce gradient noise without improving accuracy. Further ablations show that random expert selection at matched budget is about 2.5 percentage points worse, indicating that the routing signal matters, while greedy per-layer budget optimization does not improve over uniform top-k.

AI.tldr: MoE-Sieve reduces LoRA fine-tuning costs for Mixture-of-Experts models by selectively adapting only the most frequently activated "hot" experts based on routing statistics, achieving near full LoRA performance with significantly fewer parameters and training time.
AI.motivation: Standard LoRA fine-tuning applies adapters to all experts in MoE models, but profiling reveals most tokens are routed to a small subset of experts per layer, with many experts rarely activated ("cold"). This suggests wasteful parameter tuning.
AI.method: MoE-Sieve: profile routing counts on calibration set, select top-k most-routed experts per layer, apply LoRA only to those selected experts. Systematically studies expert routing across architectures and tasks.
AI.result: Tuning only top 25% routed experts per layer remains competitive with full LoRA (±1 percentage point). Reduces trainable parameters by 70-73%, checkpoint size by 71-73%, training time by up to 50%. Routing-guided selection outperforms random selection by 2.5 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: MoE-Sieve demonstrates that selective LoRA adaptation based on routing statistics is efficient and effective, reducing training costs while maintaining performance. Adapting "cold" experts may introduce gradient noise without improving accuracy.

title: The impact of sensor placement on graph-neural-network-based leakage detection
